Cardiology

571 Questions

▼
0/571 answered 0 correct
Q1

Pulsus bisiferiens is seen in all, EXCEPT:

Pulsus bisiferiens is NOT seen in TOF. It occurs in AR, AS+AR, and HOCM.
Q2

Which of the following statements about Pulsus Bisiferiensis not true:

Pulsus bisferiens has two peaks both in systole. The claim it spans systole and diastole is FALSE.
Q3

Bisfiriens pulse is seen in all Except:

TOF is NOT associated with pulsus bisferiens.
Q4

Pulsus Bisiferiens is best felt in:

Pulsus bisiferiens is best felt at the brachial artery.
Q5

Pulsus paradoxus is seen in all except :

Pulsus paradoxus is NOT characteristic of constrictive pericarditis (Kussmaul's sign occurs instead).
Q6

Pulsus paradoxus is present in all Except:

Pulsus paradoxus is not typically seen in HOCM; seen in tamponade, COPD, PE.
Q7

True about pulsus paradoxus is —

In pulsus paradoxus, stroke volume DECREASES (not increases) during inspiration.
Q8

Pulsus paradoxus is seen in (select three correct options)—

Pulsus paradoxus: tamponade (A), HOCM (C), severe asthma (E) are three correct options.
Q9

Pulsus alternans occurs in –

Pulsus alternans occurs in viral myocarditis and severe LV dysfunction (MI, heart failure).
Q10

All of the following statements about Pulsus Bigeminus are true, except:

In pulsus bigeminus, compensatory pause IS present — 'absent' is FALSE.
Q11

Pulsus Bigeminus is seen in therapy with:

Pulsus bigeminus is classically associated with digitalis toxicity.
Q12

Water hammer pulse is seen in-

Water hammer (Corrigan's) pulse is pathognomonic of Aortic Regurgitation.
Q13

Double apical impulse is seen in –

Double apical impulse: seen in HOCM — presystolic expansion + systolic impulse.
Q14

Pulse pressure is:

Pulse pressure = Systolic BP − Diastolic BP.
Q15

Wide pulse pressure may be seen in all Except:

CHF causes NARROW pulse pressure. AR, PDA, complete heart block cause wide pulse pressure.
Q16

Wide pulse pressure is seen in all except:

Aortic stenosis causes NARROW pulse pressure. Wide: PDA, AR, AV malformation.
Q17

Pulse deficit of more than 10 per minute is seen in:

Pulse deficit >10/min is characteristic of Atrial Fibrillation.
Q18

Pulse deficit may be seen in all of the following, Except :

Pulse deficit is NOT seen in bradyarrhythmias.
Q19

All of the following statements about Pulse deficit in Atrial Fibrillation are true, except:

In AF, radial pulse is LESS than ventricular rate — 'more than' is FALSE.
Q20

All of the following phases of the jugular venous pulse and their causes are correctly matched, except: (AI 2002)

'c' wave in JVP: due to tricuspid valve bulging into RA (not onset of atrial systole).
Q21

C’ Wave in Jugular Venous Pulse (JVP) waveform is due to:

'C' wave in JVP: tricuspid valve bulging into RA during ventricular systole.
Q22

Which of the following is the correct statement regarding findings in JVP:

Cannon 'a' wave = complete heart block. Giant 'v' wave = tricuspid regurgitation.
Q23

Giant ‘a’ waves in JVP occur in all except:

TR gives giant 'v' waves, NOT 'a' waves. 'a' waves require atrial contraction against closed TV.
Q24

In the JVP, ‘a’ waves are absent in :

'a' waves absent in Atrial Fibrillation (no organised atrial contraction).
Q25

‘C’ wave in JVP is due to-

'C' wave in JVP: tricuspid valve bulging into RA.
Q26

C wave in JVP indicates:

'C' wave in JVP indicates tricuspid valve bulging into the right atrium.
Q27

Typical JVP finding in cardiac tamponade –

Cardiac tamponade: absent 'y' descent. Beck's triad: hypotension + muffled heart sounds + raised JVP.
Q28

Frog sign is best related to:

Frog sign: visible neck pulsations in PSVT due to rapid cannon 'a' waves.
Q29

Erb’s Point in cardiology refers to:

Erb's point = left 3rd ICS — best site for hearing aortic regurgitation murmur.
Q30

S2 is best appreciated in:

S1 is best heard at the mitral area (cardiac apex, 5th ICS MCL).
Q31

P2 is best appreciated in :

Paradoxical splitting of S2 (P2 before A2): LBBB and severe aortic stenosis.
Q32

All of the following are true about S1, except:

P2 is loudest at 2nd left intercostal space (pulmonary area).
Q33

First heart sound is soft in all, except:

S3 gallop = early diastole, rapid ventricular filling → heart failure, DCM.
Q34

Causes of soft S1 include:

S4 gallop = presystolic, seen in reduced ventricular compliance: HTN, HOCM, ischaemia.
Q35

All of the above Loud S1 is caused by (select two correct options):

Opening snap of MS: best heard at lower left sternal border/apex.
Q36

Loud S1 in Mitral stenosis is caused by:

A2-OS interval: SHORTER = MORE severe MS (higher LA pressure).
Q37

Reverse splitting of first heart sound in heard in (select two correct options):

Loud S1 = pliable mitral stenosis (forceful valve closure due to high LAP).
Q38

Paradoxical splitting of second heart sound is seen in?

Mid-diastolic murmur with presystolic accentuation at apex = Mitral Stenosis (sinus rhythm).
Q39

Wide-split second heart sound is seen in (select two options)—

Carey Coombs murmur: soft mid-diastolic murmur in acute rheumatic carditis.
Q40

Wide-splitting of S2 is seen in (select three correct options):

Austin Flint murmur: mid-diastolic rumble at apex in severe AR (functional MS from AR jet).
Q41

Fixed splitting of S2 may be seen in all except:

Graham Steell murmur: early diastolic PR murmur due to pulmonary hypertension.
Q42

Wide fixed split S2 occurs in:

Continuous machinery murmur = PDA (patent ductus arteriosus).
Q43

Reverse splitting of S2 is seen in all except (select two options):

Dock's murmur: diastolic murmur in LAD coronary artery stenosis.
Q44

Single second heart sound is seen in (select 3 options):

Severe MS: valve area ≤1.5 cm². Normal = 4-6 cm².
Q45

Loud pulmonary component of second heart sound heard in (select two options) –

MVP: mid-systolic click + late systolic murmur at apex.
Q46

Third heart sound is seen in all except :

MVP: click/murmur move EARLIER with standing/Valsalva (↓preload → earlier prolapse).
Q47

All of the following statements about third Heart sound (S3) are true, except:

AS: harsh crescendo-decrescendo ejection systolic murmur, 2nd right ICS, radiates to carotids.
Q48

True about third heart sound is –

Severe AS: murmur peak shifts LATER in systole; A2 soft or absent.
Q49

Which of the following is true about fourth heart sound ‘S4’ :

AR: high-pitched blowing decrescendo early diastolic murmur at Erb's point.
Q50

S4 is seen in all of the following, Except:

HOCM murmur INCREASES with Valsalva and standing (↓preload worsens obstruction).
Q51

S4 is not seen in :

Most common valvular lesion in rheumatic heart disease: Mitral stenosis.
Q52

All of the following heart sounds occur shortly after S2 except:

Major Jones criteria: Carditis, Polyarthritis, Chorea, Erythema marginatum, Subcutaneous nodules.
Q53

Following is true regarding opening snap :

ASO titer >200 Todd units (adults) = recent streptococcal infection.
Q54

All of the following are diastolic sounds, Except:

Infective endocarditis most commonly affects: mitral valve > aortic > tricuspid.
Q55

Midsystolic click is characteristic feature of:

Duke criteria: 2 major, or 1 major + 3 minor, or 5 minor criteria for definite IE.
Q56

Which of the following statements is true about Click and murmur of MVP after squatting:

Osler's nodes: TENDER subcutaneous nodules on digits → SBE (immune complex deposition).
Q57

Systolic thrill in left 2nd and 3rd intercostals space may be seen in all of the following, Except:

Janeway lesions: NON-TENDER hemorrhagic macules on palms/soles → acute IE (septic emboli).
Q58

An early systolic murmur may be caused by all of the following except:

Most common organism in native valve IE: Streptococcus viridans.
Q59

All of the following murmurs may be heard in patients with aortic regurgitation except :

Most common organism in IV drug users IE: Staphylococcus aureus (tricuspid valve).
Q60

Mid-diastolic Murmur with presystolic accentuation is typically seen in:

Early prosthetic valve IE (<60 days): Staphylococcus epidermidis.
Q61

What is false in relation to Carey Coombs Murmur?

Roth spots: oval retinal haemorrhages with pale centres → IE.
Q62

Carey coomb murmur is seen in –

ASD secundum: wide FIXED splitting of S2 is the hallmark.
Q63

Rytand’s murmur is seen in –

Most common CHD: VSD (~30% of all CHD).
Q64

Which of the following best relates to Cabot-Locke murmur:

Pulmonic stenosis does NOT cause Eisenmenger's. VSD, ASD, PDA can.
Q65

Pericardial friction rub is best heard at:

Eisenmenger's: pulmonary HTN causes reversal of shunt → cyanosis.
Q66

Pericardial knock is usually present in:

TOF: VSD + pulmonic stenosis + overriding aorta + RVH. Boot-shaped heart on CXR.
Q67

A new systolic murmur after Acute myocardial infarction may be due to all of the following Except: (AIIMS May 1993)

TOF cyanotic spells: increased infundibular spasm + ↓SVR → more R→L shunting.
Q68

In which of the following condition cardiac murmur is increased on standing position?

Tet spells: knee-chest position → ↑SVR → ↓R→L shunting.
Q69

Which of the following murmurs increase with Valsalva maneuver?

Ebstein's: downward displacement of tricuspid valve into RV. Associated with WPW.
Q70

A young patient presents with a systolic murmur at the apex. The murmur increases on both handgrip and valsalva maneuver. Which of the following conditions is most likely?

TGA: aorta from RV, PA from LV. Egg-on-side CXR.
Q71

Which of the following murmurs increase with valsalva maneovre:

Coarctation: rib notching, 3-sign on CXR, radio-femoral delay. Associated with bicuspid AV, Turner's.
Q72

Continuous murmur is found in all, EXCEPT:

Dressler's syndrome: post-MI pericarditis 1-6 weeks after MI (autoimmune).
Q73

Continuous murmur is seen in all the following except:

Most common site of MI: anterior wall (LAD territory).
Q74

A continuous murmur is heard in all of the following conditions except:

ST elevation V1-V4 + reciprocal depression II/III/aVF = Anterior STEMI (LAD).
Q75

Nobel Price for invention of Electrocardiogram was awarded to:

ST elevation II/III/aVF = Inferior STEMI (RCA/LCx).
Q76

Normal Axis of the heart is best described as being between:

STEMI thrombolysis: door-to-needle ≤30 min. Window: within 12 hours of onset.
Q77

Normal heart axis is:

Cardiac troponin I and T: most sensitive and specific biomarkers for MI.
Q78

-30 TO -60 degree axis deviation indicates:

CK-MB: rises 4-6 hrs, peaks 24 hrs, normalises 48-72 hrs. Best for detecting reinfarction.
Q79

-30 TO -60 degree axis deviation is seen in:

Troponin remains elevated 7-10 days — NOT useful for detecting reinfarction.
Q80

Left Axis Deviation is seen as:

Biomarker of choice for reinfarction: CK-MB (normalises then rises again).
Q81

P wave is due to:

Streptokinase: antigenic, cannot be re-used within 6 months-2 years.
Q82

The normal P wave is inverted in lead :

Primary PCI preferred over thrombolysis when available within 90 minutes.
Q83

The normal P wave is biphasic in lead:

IABP in cardiogenic shock: reduces afterload, augments diastolic coronary perfusion.
Q84

Absent P Wave is seen in:

Most common mechanical complication of MI: Acute MR due to papillary muscle rupture.
Q85

Pseudo-P-Pulmonale is typically seen in:

Free wall rupture: days 3-5 post-MI → acute tamponade, electromechanical dissociation.
Q86

A patient in regular rhythm presents with absent P waves on ECG. Leads II, III and AVF reveal a Saw- Tooth Pattern. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis:

Post-MI VSD: harsh pansystolic murmur + O2 step-up in RV. Emergency surgery.
Q87

A wide and notched P wave is typically seen in:

RBBB + LAHB = bifascicular block → risk of complete heart block → temporary pacemaker.
Q88

QRS complex indicates-

Complete heart block: transcutaneous/transvenous pacing.
Q89

A QRS duration between 100 and 120 milliseconds suggests all of the following, Except:

Hypertensive emergency: IV labetalol/nicardipine/nitroprusside. Reduce MAP ≤25% in first hour.
Q90

Wide QRS complex [≥ 0.12 seconds] may be seen in all of the following EXCEPT:

Hypertensive emergency in pregnancy: IV labetalol or hydralazine.
Q91

Wide QRS complex is typically seen in:

ACE inhibitors CONTRAINDICATED in bilateral renal artery stenosis.
Q92

Low QRS voltage on ECG indicates:

First-line HF: ACE inhibitor/ARB + beta-blocker + aldosterone antagonist.
Q93

The ECG of a 40 year old male was recorded using standard bipolar limb leads. The sum of voltage of the three standard leads was found to be 5 millivolts. This indicates:

NYHA Class IV HF + EF ≤35% + LBBB + QRS ≥130 ms → CRT.
Q94

Low QRS voltage on ECG with left ventricular hypertrophy on Echocardiography suggests a diagnosis of:

Digoxin toxicity: yellow-green haloes, nausea, bradycardia, arrhythmias. Toxic level >2 ng/mL.
Q95

In LVH, SV1 + RV 6 is more than ________ mm?

Digoxin: ↑vagal tone → slows AV node. Na-K ATPase inhibition → positive inotropy.
Q96

Tall T waves on ECG are seen in:

Amiodarone side effects: pulmonary toxicity, thyroid dysfunction, corneal deposits, hepatotoxicity.
Q97

ST elevation is seen in all of the following conditions except :

Long QT → torsades de pointes → VF → sudden death.
Q98

All of the following may cause ST segment elevation on EKG, except :

WPW: delta wave + short PR + wide QRS. Bundle of Kent bypasses AV node.
Q99

What is diagnostic of fresh myocardial infarction in ECG :

WPW: radiofrequency ablation is definitive. AVOID digoxin and verapamil.
Q100

Reciprocal changes in ECG in patients with inferior wall myocardial infarction are seen in which leads-

Atrial flutter: sawtooth waves at 300/min, 2:1 AV block → ventricular rate ~150/min.
Q101

QT prolongation is seen in all, except :

AF cardioversion: <48 hours → can cardiovert directly. ≥48 hrs → anticoagulate 3 weeks first.
Q102

QT interval is shortened in:

HOCM: asymmetric septal hypertrophy, AD, most common SCD cause in young athletes.
Q103

Hypercalcemia in ECG is diagnosed by:

HOCM: SAM of mitral valve on echo → LVOT obstruction.
Q104

Hypocalcemia is characterized by all of the following features except-

Dilated CMP: ischaemia most common overall. Viral (Coxsackievirus B) = most common non-ischaemic.
Q105

All are ECG changes in hypokalemia, except:

Restrictive CMP: most commonly amyloidosis.
Q106

Which of the following represents the earliest ECG Change in a patient with Hypokalemia:

Constrictive pericarditis: Kussmaul's sign, pericardial knock, square root sign.
Q107

The following ECG findings are seen in Hypokalemia:

Cardiac tamponade: Beck's triad, pulsus paradoxus >10 mmHg, electrical alternans on ECG.
Q108

All of the following are the electrocardiographic features of Hyperkalemia, except-

Electrical alternans = large pericardial effusion with tamponade.
Q109

All of the following may occur due to hyperkalemia, except:

Pericardiocentesis: subxiphoid approach directed toward left shoulder.
Q110

All of the following electrocardiographic findings may represent manifestations of digitalis intoxication, except:

Acute pericarditis: diffuse saddle-shaped ST elevation, PR depression.
Q111

Osborne waves in ECG are seen in:

Most common cause of acute pericarditis: viral (Coxsackievirus A and B).
Q112

Epsilon wave is seen in:

MVP: most common valvular abnormality in general population.
Q113

All of the following conditions are more commonly associated with LBBB pattern than RBBB Pattern on ECG, Except:

Barlow's syndrome = MVP (myxomatous mitral valve disease).
Q114

Idiopathic degeneration of the proximal bundle branch fibres is known as:

Libman-Sacks endocarditis: nonbacterial verrucous endocarditis in SLE.
Q115

Brugada Syndrome is associated with:

Carcinoid heart disease: right-sided lesions (TR, PS) due to serotonin-mediated fibrosis.
Q116

Which of the following arrhythmias is most frequent in Holiday heart syndrome:

Paradoxical embolism: clot passes right-to-left via PFO or ASD → systemic embolism.
Q117

Holiday heart syndrome is the term used for cardiotoxicity produced by:

Normal LVEF: 55-70%. EF <40% = reduced EF heart failure.
Q119

Following ECG manifestations are seen in acute pericarditis except -

LVEF most accurately measured by Simpson's biplane method on echo.
Q120

In left sided massive pneumothorax, ECG shows all, except:

BNP: best biomarker for diagnosing and monitoring heart failure.
Q121

Which of the following ECG changes is least likely in a patient with left pneumothorax :

Most common cause of TR: functional (RV dilation from pulmonary hypertension).
Q122

Atrial fibrillation may occur in all the following conditions, except-

Cor pulmonale: RVH/failure from pulmonary disease.
Q123

Most common in Arrhythmia in ICU Patients is:

WHO Groups of pulmonary hypertension: I=PAH, II=left heart, III=lung, IV=CTEPH, V=unclear.
Q124

A chronic alcoholic develops palpitations suddenly after alcohol binge. Which of the following arrhythmia is most commonly associated with alcohol binge in the alcoholics?

Idiopathic PAH: young women, BMPR2 mutation.
Q125

Which of the following agents should not be used for oral anticoagulation in high risk patients with Atrial Fibrillation:

Differential cyanosis (lower body blue, upper body pink) = PDA with Eisenmenger's.
Q126

All of the following are true about Atrial Fibrillation, Except:

TOF ECG: right axis deviation + RVH pattern.
Q127

A person with mitral regurgitation and atrial fibrillation presents with syncope. On examination the person has a heart rate of 55. What is the most probable cause?

Ebstein's: massive cardiomegaly, RA enlargement, WPW association.
Q128

In a patient with chronic atrial fibrillation with a regular beat of 60/min, the most probable cause is: (AIIMS May 01)

Most common cyanotic CHD in neonates: TGA.
Q129

Drug of choice in PSVT is :

Most common cyanotic CHD after neonatal period: TOF.
Q130

Drug of choice for maintenance therapy in PSVT IS:

ASD: wide fixed splitting of S2 is the hallmark.
Q131

Ventricular tachycardia is defined as:

Secundum ASD: 75% of all ASDs, at fossa ovalis.
Q132

Sustained VT is defined as VT that persists for:

Primum ASD: associated with cleft mitral valve, Down syndrome.
Q133

All of the following features can differentiate between ventricular tachycardia and supraventricular tachycardia except:

VSD: most common CHD. Loud pansystolic murmur at LLSB.
Q134

In a patient with wide-complex tachycardia, the presence of all of the following in the ECG indicates ventricular tachycardia except:

Maladie de Roger = small restrictive VSD with loud murmur but minimal haemodynamic impact.
Q135

Board complex tachycardia, due to ventricular tachycardia is suggested by all except:

PDA: continuous machinery murmur. Indomethacin closes PDA in premature infants.
Q136

Broad complex tachycardia due to ventricular tachycardia is suggested by all of the following except: (NBE)

Coarctation: postductal most common. Associated with bicuspid AV, Turner's syndrome.
Q137

Ventricular Tachycardia is represented by all, Except:

Coarctation: rib notching from enlarged intercostal arterial collaterals.
Q138

Which of the following ECG features are not seen in patients with ventricular tachycardia:

Long QT: LQT1 (swimming), LQT2 (auditory triggers), LQT3 (sleep/rest).
Q139

Which of the following statements about Premature Ventricular Beats is false:

Brugada: RBBB pattern + ST elevation V1-V3 → VF risk. SE Asian males.
Q140

All of the following are features of Premature Ventricular Complexes, Except:

AVNRT: most common cause of paroxysmal SVT.
Q141

Accelerated Idioventricular Rhythm (AIVR) is the most common arrhythmia associated with:

Adenosine: drug of choice for acute termination of SVT/PSVT. Half-life ~10 seconds.
Q142

Which of the following is not true about Torsades de pointes?

Verapamil CONTRAINDICATED in WPW + AF.
Q143

Feature of Torsade de pointes is:

VT: wide QRS >0.12 sec, AV dissociation, capture/fusion beats.
Q144

Congenital long QT syndrome can lead to:

AV dissociation is the most reliable criterion differentiating VT from SVT with aberrancy.
Q145

Torsade-de-pointes is caused by :

ICD: EF ≤35% + NYHA II-III despite optimal medical therapy.
Q146

The most common accessory pathways leading to WPW syndrome is:

Pacemaker syndrome: VVI pacing → AV dissociation → retrograde VA conduction.
Q147

Bundle of KENT is involved in which of the following disease?

CRT: EF ≤35% + LBBB + QRS ≥130 ms + NYHA III-IV.
Q148

Type B pre excitation in WPW syndrome is caused by:

Most common arrhythmia worldwide: Atrial fibrillation.
Q149

The most common reentrant tachycardia associated with WPW syndrome is:

AF: irregular irregular pulse, absent 'a' wave JVP, variable S1, pulse deficit.
Q150

All are ECG findings in Wolf Parkinson’s white syndrome, except:

Most common cause of AF in India: Rheumatic heart disease.
Q151

All of the following statements about WPW syndrome are true, Except:

WPW: short PR, delta wave, wide QRS.
Q152

The drug of choice in patients with Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome with atrial fibrillation is-

Normal PR interval: 0.12-0.20 seconds.
Q153

A Patient with short PR interval and Delta waves on ECG presents to the emergency department with Atrial Fibrillation and rapid ventricular rate. He is hemodynamically stable. All of the following agents may be used in the management, except:

1st degree AV block: prolonged PR >0.20 sec, all P waves conducted.
Q154

Which of the following is the treatment of choice for Atrial Fibrillation in patients with WPW Syndrome who are hemodynamically unstable:

Wenckebach (Mobitz I): progressive PR prolongation until dropped beat. Site = AV node.
Q155

Radiofrequency Ablation of the bypass tract is done for:

Mobitz II: constant PR then sudden dropped beat → pacemaker required.
Q156

Sinus Bradycardia is defined as heart rate of?

2:1 AV block: treat as Mobitz II (pacemaker).
Q157

All of the following are features of Mobitz Type I block, except:

Complete AV block: P waves unrelated to QRS. Ventricular escape rate 30-40/min.
Q158

Constant PR interval is seen in:

Holter monitor: 24-hr ambulatory ECG for intermittent arrhythmias.
Q159

All of the following may be seen in Mobitz Type II second degree Heart block, except:

Pathological Q waves (>0.04 sec, >25% of R height) = old MI.
Q160

True statement regarding Atrioventricular block with atrial tachycardia is:

Hypokalaemia ECG: U waves prominent, flat T waves, prolonged QU interval.
Q161

All of the following are associated with Atrioventricular Block, except:

Hyperkalaemia ECG: peaked T waves (earliest) → wide QRS → sine wave → VF.
Q162

Most common cause of Acquired Complete Heart Block in children is

Hypocalcaemia ECG: prolonged QT interval.
Q163

In the treatment of severe bradycardia, all of the following can be best modality of treatment except:

Hypercalcaemia ECG: shortened QT interval.
Q164

All of the following are true about Right Ventricular Infarcts, Except:

LVH (Sokolow-Lyon): S in V1 + R in V5/V6 ≥35 mm.
Q165

All the following are features of right sided heart failure, Except:

Normal QRS duration: 0.06-0.10 sec. BBB: ≥0.12 sec.
Q166

Increased hydrostatic pressure in left sided congestive heart failure causes -

LBBB: broad notched R in I, aVL, V5-V6; deep S in V1-V3. New LBBB + chest pain = STEMI equivalent.
Q167

All of the following are true about Right Ventricular Infarcts, Except

RBBB: rSR' (M-shaped) in V1, wide S in I, V5-V6.
Q168

Which of the following is not a major Framingham criteria in CHF:

LAHB: left axis deviation (-30° to -90°), small Q in I/aVL.
Q169

Left ventricular Hypertropy is caused by all, Except:

Sgarbossa criteria: diagnose MI in LBBB.
Q170

CCF is associated with increase in all the following except:

Exercise stress test positive: ≥1 mm horizontal/downsloping ST depression at J+0.08 sec.
Q171

Positive hepatojugular reflux is found in conditions except:

Bruce protocol: most common exercise stress test, 3-minute stages.
Q172

Positive Hepatojugular Reflux is found in all of the following conditions, except:

Most sensitive test for CAD: nuclear stress test (thallium/sestamibi).
Q173

Enlarged Pulsatile liver with ascitis is typically seen in:

Coronary angiography: gold standard for CAD. Significant stenosis ≥70%.
Q174

Left atrial filling pressure closely approximates-

FFR <0.80: haemodynamically significant stenosis requiring revascularisation.
Q175

A Patient comes with sudden respiratory distress. On examination, bilateral basal crepts are present over chest suggestive of pulmonary edema. Alveolar wedge pressure is normal. The likely cause is-

CABG preferred: left main disease, 3-vessel CAD, diabetes with multivessel disease.
Q176

Normal PCWP with pulmonary oedema is seen in-

PCI preferred: 1-2 vessel disease, STEMI.
Q177

Pulmonary edema associated with normal PCWP is observed. Which of these is not a cause:

Most common site of atherosclerosis: abdominal aorta bifurcation.
Q178

Assays of which of the following biomarkers is most commonly used in the diagnosis of Heart failure

AAA: elective repair when >5.5 cm.
Q179

Which of the following biomarkers has been shown to correlate best with pulmonary vascular resistance in patients with heart failure:

Type A dissection (ascending aorta): emergency surgery. Type B: medical management.
Q180

Which of the following biomarkers is released from ventricular myocytes:

Marfan's: aortic root dilation/AR, MVP. FBN1 gene mutation.
Q181

All of the following are used in the initial management of acute life threatening cardiogenic pulmonary edema, except:

Takayasu's arteritis: granulomatous aortoarteritis in young Asian women.
Q182

All of the following medications may be used in congestive cardiac failure, Except

Leriche syndrome: aortoiliac occlusion → absent femoral pulses, buttock claudication, impotence.
Q183

All of the following are true about starting beta-blocker therapy in cases of CHF, Except

ABI <0.9 = peripheral arterial disease. <0.4 = critical limb ischaemia.
Q184

Treatment of choice for patients with Congestive Heart Failure and an ejection fraction of < 40 percent is:

DVT diagnosis: venous duplex ultrasound gold standard.
Q185

All of the following medications have shown to lower mortality in patients with CHF, Except:

PE ECG: tachycardia most common. Classic (rare): S1Q3T3.
Q186

In a patient with chronic congestive cardiac failure, all of the following drugs prolong survival except :

PE diagnosis: CTPA is gold standard.
Q187

All of the following statements about digitalis are true except :

Heparin: activates antithrombin III. Monitored by aPTT.
Q188

Digoxin toxicity is enhanced by all, except:

Warfarin: inhibits vitamin K-dependent factors II, VII, IX, X. Monitored by INR.
Q189

All of the following may precipitate digitalis toxicity except :

Thrombolysis for massive PE with haemodynamic instability: alteplase 100 mg over 2 hours.
Q190

Treatment of digoxin over dose includes administration of all of the following except :

Venous ulcers: medial ankle, hyperpigmentation. Arterial: lateral/toe, absent pulses.
Q191

Following are used in treatment of digitalis toxicity except-

Mid-diastolic murmur with presystolic accentuation = Mitral Stenosis.
Q192

All the following are radiological features of Chronic Cor pulmonale except :

Vitum's sign: visible venous pulsations in neck — TR, constrictive pericarditis.
Q193

Which of the following is the least common cause of True Valvular Mitral Stenosis

Carvallo's sign: TR murmur increases with inspiration.
Q194

Area of mitral orifice in adults is:

Traube's double sound (pistol-shot) over femoral artery = sign of AR.
Q195

Mitral stenosis is associated with :

Duroziez's sign: to-and-fro murmur over compressed femoral artery = significant AR.
Q196

Mid diastolic Murmur with presystolic accentuation is typically seen in:

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

de Musset's sign: head bobbing with each heartbeat = severe AR.
Q197

All of the following may be seen as severity of mitral stenosis increases Except

Müller's sign: uvular pulsations = AR.
Q198

Severity of mitral stenosis is assessed by:

Hill's sign in AR: popliteal SBP exceeds brachial SBP by >20 mmHg.
Q199

Which clinical sign is suggestive of elevated pulmonary wedge presure in mitral stenosis?

Quincke's sign: capillary pulsations at nail bed = significant AR.
Q200

Double Density sign in Mitral Stenosis is a sign of enlargement of:

Rosenbach's sign: hepatic pulsations = TR.
Q201

Absolute Contraindication for Percutaneous Balloon Mitral Valvotomy include the following except

Kussmaul's sign: paradoxical JVP rise with inspiration = constrictive pericarditis.
Q202

Mitral valve replacement is recommended in patients with

Beck's triad: muffled heart sounds + hypotension + raised JVP = cardiac tamponade.
Q203

Severity of Mitral Regurgitation may be judged by

Pericardial friction rub: triphasic scratching sound, best heard leaning forward in expiration.
Q204

The severity of mitral regurgitation is decided by all of the following clinical findings except:

Cardiac tamponade: pericardiocentesis is emergency treatment.
Q205

A 59 year old man with severe myxomatous mitral regurgitation is asymptomatic, with a left ventricular ejection fraction of 45% and an endsystolic diameter index of 2.9 cm/m2. The most appropriate treatment is

Constrictive pericarditis: pericardiectomy is definitive treatment. TB most common cause in India.
Q206

A young asymptomatic female is observed to have a Midsystolic Click on routine examination. Valves are likely to show

Acute RF: preceded by streptococcal PHARYNGITIS 2-4 weeks prior.
Q207

Barlow's syndrome refers to

Streptococcal throat: treat with penicillin V for 10 days to prevent RF.
Q208

All of the following statements about mitral valve prolapse are true except:

Aschoff bodies: pathognomonic of rheumatic carditis — perivascular granulomas.
Q209

True regarding Mitral valve prolapse syndrome is all, EXCEPT-

Anitschkow cells: modified macrophages in Aschoff bodies.
Q210

All of the following are true for mitral valve prolapse, except-

McCallum's patch: endocardial thickening in posterior LA wall in rheumatic fever.
Q211

All of the following are true about Mid-late systolic click of MVP, EXCEPT

Lutembacher's syndrome: mitral stenosis + ASD.
Q212

Which of the following CONGENITAL Heart DISEASES is associated with MVP

Still's murmur: innocent vibratory systolic murmur in children at LLSB.
Q213

A 26 yr old asymptomatic woman is found to have arrhythmias and a systolic murmur associated with midsystolic clicks; which investigation would you use

Most common cause of MR in Western countries: MVP (myxomatous degeneration).
Q214

A female patient Chandani develops chest pain which is not associated with exercise and chest auscultation shows multiple non ejection clicks. The investigation which is used to diagnose the disease is :

Chronic MR: volume overload → LV dilatation. Pansystolic murmur at apex, radiates to axilla.
Q215

Angina pectoris and Syncope are most likely to be associated with:

Acute severe MR: sudden L→R shunting → pulmonary oedema.
Q216

A 63 year old man present with a triad of angina, syncope and congestive heart failure. Which of the following valvular heart lesion can be suspected :

Severe AS: valve area <1.0 cm², mean gradient >40 mmHg, peak velocity >4 m/sec.
Q217

Which of the following valvular heart diseases is most commonly associated with sudden death:

Severe AS triad: angina (5 yr), syncope (3 yr), dyspnoea/HF (2 yr survival).
Q218

In severe aortic stenosis true finding is :

Chronic severe AR: volume overload → LV dilatation. Wide pulse pressure, water hammer pulse.
Q219

Which of the following physical signs is seen in a patient with severe aortic stenosis:

Acute severe AR: sudden pulmonary oedema. Emergency surgery.
Q220

All are features of aortic stenosis except

Acute pericarditis: concave-up ST elevation, PR depression, pleuritic chest pain, friction rub.
Q221

Calcification of the aortic valve is seen in :

LDH1 > LDH2 flip in MI. LDH peaks 24-48 hours, remains elevated 7-10 days.
Q222

Sub-valvular Aortic Stenosis is known to be associated with all of the following, except:

Earliest sensitive MI marker: Myoglobin (rises 1-2 hrs). Most specific: Troponin I/T.
Q223

The most common complication of Sub-valvular Aortic Stenosis is:

Myoglobin: rises 1-2 hrs, normalises 24 hrs. Useful for early detection, non-specific.
Q224

AS is called severe when the aortic valve area is

GRACE score: risk stratification in NSTEMI/UA.
Q225

Acute Aortic regurgitation is seen in all the following except:

Target LDL in high-risk CAD: <70 mg/dL (newer guidelines aim for <55 mg/dL).
Q226

Aortic regurgitation does NOT occur in :

Statins: HMG-CoA reductase inhibitors, reduce LDL, pleiotropic effects.
Q228

A 35 year old athlete has height 184 cm., arm span 194 cm., pulse rate 64/min., BP 148/64 mm Hg. Chest auscultation reveals long diastolic murmur over right 2nd intercostal space on routine examination. The probable diagnosis is :

Familial hypercholesterolaemia: AD, LDL receptor defect. Homozygous: TC >800, childhood MI.
Q229

A young basketball player with ht 188cm & arm span 197cm has a diastolic murmur best heard in second right intercostal space, likely cause of murmur is

Metabolic syndrome: 3 of 5 ATP III criteria.
Q230

Duroziez's sign is seen in

Syndrome X: angina + normal coronaries + positive stress test = microvascular dysfunction.
Q231

LVH is commonly seen with :

ARVC: fibrofatty RV replacement, VT with LBBB morphology, epsilon wave on ECG.
Q232

Which of the following is contraindicated in the treatment of acute severe aortic regurgitation

Lyme disease cardiac: AV block, myopericarditis. Treat with antibiotics.
Q233

Peripheral pulmonary stenosis may occur at the following sites :

Amyloid heart: restrictive CMP, sparkling myocardium on echo, low voltage ECG.
Q234

Peripheral pulmonic stenosis is associated with (select two options):

Peripartum CMP: LV systolic dysfunction last month or within 5 months of delivery.
Q235

In pulmonary stenosis the interval between first heart sound (S1) and Systolic ejection click:

Tako-Tsubo: transient apical ballooning, emotional stress trigger, postmenopausal women.
Q236

Ejection click of pulmonary stenosis is better heard in

WPW: delta wave, short PR, wide QRS. Bundle of Kent bypasses AV node.
Q237

The most common cause of tricuspid regurgitation is secondary to –

LGL syndrome: short PR, normal QRS (no delta wave). James fibers.
Q238

Hepatomegaly with liver pulsation indicates:

Mahaim fibers: connect AV node to ventricle → LBBB pattern tachycardia.
Q239

Vitum's Sign is seen in

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Prinzmetal angina: ST elevation during episode, normal coronaries, vasospasm.
Q240

Carvallo's sign is seen in

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Prinzmetal angina: calcium channel blockers are treatment of choice. Beta-blockers contraindicated.
Q241

Carvallo's Sign representing a Diastolic Murmur that increases on inspiration is seen in

Chalcosis = copper deposition in tissue. Siderosis = iron. Calcinosis = calcium. Named for the metal.
Q242

All of the following are risk factors for atherosclerosis except :

HFE gene is located on the short arm of chromosome 6.
Q243

Best predictor for future risk of cardiovascular events, amongst the following is:

Haemochromatosis: arthritis + hyperpigmentation + hypogonadism (bronze diabetes triad).
Q244

Which of the following is the best marker to predict future cardiac events

Haemochromatosis: phlebotomy (venesection) is treatment of choice. Desferrioxamine NOT first choice.
Q245

The amino acid which is associated with atherosclerosis is :

Earliest phenotypic manifestation of hereditary haemochromatosis: increased transferrin saturation.
Q246

The risk of coronary artery disease is associated with an elevated level of

First joint involved in haemochromatosis: 2nd and 3rd metacarpophalangeal joints.
Q247

The protein which has structural homology with plasminogen and is responsible for myocardial infarction and stroke

Bronzing in haemochromatosis: increased iron in the dermis (melanin also deposited).
Q248

Risk factors for coronary artery disease (CAD) (select two options)–

Frequency of organ involvement in haemochromatosis: Hepatomegaly > DM > Arthropathy > Cardiac.
Q249

Predisposing factors for coronary artery disease include, all Except:

First organ affected in haemochromatosis: Liver (hepatomegaly is earliest/most common manifestation).
Q250

Raised serum level of lipoprotein-(a) is a predictor of

In diabetic cirrhotic with skin hyperpigmentation: serum ferritin elevated in haemochromatosis.
Q251

Which of the following increases the susceptibility to coronary artery disease :

Haemochromatosis: phlebotomy is treatment of choice (NOT desferrioxamine as first line).
Q252

In an old patient, the best indicator of probability of developing cardiovascular disease can be calculated by–

HBV: most common cause of HCC worldwide.
Q253

Most important predictor of coronary artery disease is:

Inherited as autosomal recessive disorder — statement about complete penetrance is FALSE; penetrance is incomplete.
Q254

An association of which of the following bacteria has been suggested with atherosclerosis:

Chalcosis = copper deposition in tissue (from Greek 'chalkos' = copper).
Q255

The most important modifiable risk factor for atherosclerosis is:

HFE gene is located on the short arm of chromosome 6 (6p21.3).
Q256

According to the ATP III Classification Desired levels of total cholesterol is:

Arthritis + hyperpigmentation + hypogonadism = Haemochromatosis (bronze diabetes triad).
Q257

All of the following dietary goals are recommended for patients with high risk of coronary heart disease, Except:

Haemochromatosis: desferrioxamine is NOT the treatment of choice — phlebotomy is. Desferrioxamine is used when phlebotomy not possible (e.g., anaemia).
Q258

Which of the following dietary interventions has shown to reduces mortality in patients with coronary heart disease.–

Earliest phenotypic manifestation of hereditary haemochromatosis: increased transferrin saturation.
Q259

A patient with hypertriglyceridemia is treated with Omega 3 polyunsaturated fatty acids. Treatment with omega-3- polyunsaturated fatty acids, will have the following effect on lipid profile:

First joint involved in haemochromatosis: 2nd and 3rd metacarpophalangeal (MCP) joints.
Q260

Which of the following statements about Atherosclerosis is true:

Bronzing in haemochromatosis: due to increased iron deposition in the dermis (plus melanin).
Q261

Which of the following is not true about metabolic syndrome?

Frequency of organ involvement: Hepatomegaly > Diabetes Mellitus > Arthropathy > Cardiac Involvement.
Q262

According to the NCEP-ATP III Definition of Metabolic Syndrome, all of the following criteria for 'Men' are correct, EXCEPT

First organ affected in haemochromatosis: Liver (skin and liver are earliest sites).
Q263

WHO Rose Questionnaire is used for

In cirrhosis with diabetes and skin pigmentation: serum ferritin is elevated (haemochromatosis screening).
Q264

In stable angina:

All statements about hereditary haemochromatosis are true EXCEPT: desferroxamine is the treatment of choice (phlebotomy is).
Q265

Episodes of Stable Angina Pectoris typically last for

All are seen in haemochromatosis EXCEPT: desferrioxamine is the treatment of choice. Phlebotomy is first-line.
Q266

Wellen's syndrome suggests:

True about haemochromatosis: inherited as autosomal recessive disorder.

Pulmonology

337 Questions

▼
0/337 answered 0 correct
Q1

All of the following types of Respiratory Failures are correctly matched, except:

Consolidation: dull percussion, bronchial breathing, increased vocal fremitus.
Q2

False statement about type I respiratory failure is:

Most common cause of CAP: Streptococcus pneumoniae.
Q3

In ICU setting patients suffering from which of the following respiratory pathology is most predisposed for CO₂ narcosis?

Atypical pneumonia: Mycoplasma, Chlamydophila, Legionella. No consolidation on CXR.
Q4

In type - II respiratory failure, there is :

Legionella: cooling towers, hyponatraemia, diarrhoea. Treat with fluoroquinolones/azithromycin.
Q5

Type III respiratory failure occurs due to:

PCP: AIDS-defining. CD4 <200. Bilateral ground-glass opacities. Treat with TMP-SMX.
Q6

Type IV respiratory failure occurs due to:

TB: primary infection → Ghon complex. Reactivation in lung apices.
Q7

Respiratory acidosis is primarily recognized by:

TB diagnosis gold standard: AFB smear + Lowenstein-Jensen culture.
Q8

A patient with salicylic acid poisoning has the following arterial blood gas analysis report : pH = 7.12 : pCO₂ = 18mmHg; HCO₃ = 12mmol/L. The resulting acid base abnormality can be best labeled as:

DOTs: directly observed therapy is cornerstone of TB control.
Q9

In a patient PO₂ is 85 mmHg, PCO₂ – 50mmHg, pH is 7.2 and HCO₃ is 32 meq/l is suffering from :** (AIIMS

TB regimen: HRZE × 2 months + HR × 4 months.
Q10

A patient presents with following parameters pH 7.5, p CO₂ 30 mmHg, pO₂ 102 mmHg and HCO₃ 16 meq/l. Which of the following correctly describes the compensatory mechanism:

Isoniazid: peripheral neuropathy (prevented by pyridoxine/B6), hepatotoxicity.
Q11

The acid base status of a patient reveals a pH = 7.46 and pCO₂=30 mm Hg. The patient has a partially compensated primary:

Rifampicin: orange-red discolouration of secretions, enzyme inducer.
Q12

A patient was admited to the casualty with hysteria and anxiety. Estimation of arterial plasma showed PH of 7.53; HCO₃ of 22 meq and Pco₂ of 27 mm H g. which of the following is the patient likely to have?

Pyrazinamide: hyperuricaemia, hepatotoxicity.
Q13

Arterial blood gas analysis of a patient reveals pH 7.2, HCO₃ 36mmol/L, pCO₂ 60mm of Hg. The abnormality is :

Ethambutol: optic neuritis — monitor visual acuity monthly.
Q14

A 40 year old male develops excessive hyperventilation. ABG reveals pH- 7.5, pCO₂ 24 mmHg, PO₂ 88 mm of Hg. Treatment is :

Streptomycin: ototoxicity, nephrotoxicity. Avoid in pregnancy.
Q15

An ABG analysis shows : pH 7.2, raised pCO₂, decreased HCO₃; diagnosis is :

MDR-TB: resistant to INH and rifampicin.
Q16

A 10 year old boy with short stature presents with polyuria & polydipsia. Laboratory values reveal a pH of 7.4; HCO₃ of 17; Na⁺ 140; pCO₂ 32; K⁺ 4.9; Cl⁻ 112. Likely acid base abnormality is:

Miliary TB: haematogenous dissemination → millet seed nodules bilaterally on CXR.
Q17

Blood gas measurements of a patient shows the following values pH 7.2, pCO₂ 80 mm Hg, pO₂ 46 mm Hg. Which of the following could be the most probable diagnosis:

Most common site of extrapulmonary TB in India: cervical lymph nodes.
Q18

The blood gas parameters: pH 7.58, pCO₂ 23 mm Hg pO₂ 300 mm Hg and oxygen saturation 60% are most consistent with:

TB pleural effusion: exudative, lymphocytic, high ADA (>40 IU/L).
Q19

Which of the following is not a restrictive pulmonary disease :

Empyema necessitans: TB empyema tracking through chest wall.
Q20

All of the following are Obstructive Lung Diseases, except:

COPD: FEV1/FVC <0.70 post-bronchodilator. Not fully reversible.
Q21

All the following are true about Obstructive lung disease except:

COPD GOLD staging: GOLD 1 (FEV1 ≥80%), GOLD 2 (50-80%), GOLD 3 (30-50%), GOLD 4 (<30%).
Q22

All of the following are characteristic feature of obstructive pulmonary disease, except:

COPD: pink puffer (emphysema type) vs blue bloater (chronic bronchitis type).
Q23

Tiffeneau-Pinelli refers to which of the following?

Chronic bronchitis: productive cough ≥3 months in each of 2 consecutive years.
Q24

FEV₁/FVC is reduced in case of :

Emphysema: permanent enlargement of air spaces distal to terminal bronchioles with wall destruction.
Q25

FEV₁/FVC is decreased in:

Centriacinar emphysema: smoking-related, affects respiratory bronchioles.
Q26

FEV₁/FVC decreased in all of the following except:

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Panacinar emphysema: alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, lower lobe predominant.
Q27

Pulmonary function changes in acute bronchial asthma in untreated patient :

Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency: PiZZ phenotype, emphysema + liver cirrhosis.
Q28

Features of restrictive lung disease is:

COPD exacerbation: SABA + SAMA, systemic steroids, antibiotics if purulent sputum.
Q29

The abnormal preoperative pulmonary function test in a patient with severe kyphoscoliosis includes:

LTOT in COPD: PaO2 ≤55 mmHg (or ≤60 with cor pulmonale). ≥15 hr/day.
Q30

A 28 years old woman having limited cutaneous scleroderma for the last 10 years complains of shortness of breath for last one month. Her pulmonary functions tests (PFT) are as follows : * **PFT Data:** * FVC: Observed 2.63, Predicted 2.82 * FEV1%: Observed 88%, Predicted 80% * DLCO: Observed 5.26, Predicted 16.3 **What is the most likely diagnosis in this case ?

Asthma: reversible airflow obstruction, hyperresponsiveness, FEV1/FVC <0.70.
Q31

A patient presents with decreased vital capacity and total lung volume. What is the most probable diagnosis?

Asthma step-up therapy: Step 1 SABA PRN → Step 2 low ICS → Step 3 ICS+LABA.
Q32

All are decreased in infiltrative lung disease, except:

Most common asthma trigger in children: respiratory viral infections.
Q33

Carbon monoxide diffusion capacity decreases in all, except : (AI-2003)

Drug-induced asthma: aspirin/NSAIDs (leukotriene pathway), beta-blockers.
Q34

Diffusion capacity for carbon monoxide is decreased in all of the following Except:

Samter's triad: asthma + nasal polyps + aspirin sensitivity.
Q35

Decreased maximum mid-expiratory flow rate indicates obstruction in :

Status asthmaticus: nebulised salbutamol + ipratropium, IV magnesium, IV steroids.
Q36

All of the following are true about intrinsic Asthma, Except:

IV magnesium: bronchodilator in severe asthma. Mechanism: calcium antagonism.
Q37

All of the following statements about intrinsic allergic Asthma are true, Except:

Asthma PFT: ↓FEV1, ↓FVC, ↓FEV1/FVC, ↑RV, ↑TLC. Reversible with bronchodilator.
Q38

Asprin sensitive asthma is associated with which of the following?

Methacholine challenge: PC20 <8 mg/mL = airway hyperresponsiveness → asthma.
Q39

Samter’s triad refers to the association of Asprin sensitive asthma and

Bronchiectasis: CF, immune deficiency, post-infectious (TB, pertussis), Kartagener's.
Q40

Bronchial Asthma is characterized by all of the following, Except:

Kartagener's: bronchiectasis + situs inversus + sinusitis. Primary ciliary dyskinesia.
Q41

All of the following are true about Asthma, Except:

Bronchiectasis: postural drainage, antibiotics, bronchodilators.
Q42

True about Asthma except:

Cystic fibrosis: CFTR gene, chromosome 7q31, autosomal recessive.
Q43

Feature of Acute severe Asthma include all of the following, Except:

CF most common mutation: ΔF508. Most common lethal hereditary disease in Caucasians.
Q44

Life threatening features of asthma are the following except:

CF diagnosis: sweat chloride >60 mEq/L.
Q45

In a patient with bronchial asthma silent chest signifies

CF: Pseudomonas infections, exocrine pancreatic insufficiency, male infertility.
Q46

An Adult with asthma presents with asthma symptoms every day and wakes up in the night approximately 2 to 3 days in a week. He can be classified as having :

Most common cause of bronchiectasis in India: Post-TB.
Q47

Universal finding in Asthma is

Sarcoidosis: non-caseating granulomas, bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy.
Q48

In a Patient with clinical signs of Asthma which of the following tests will confirm the diagnosis:

Sarcoidosis: elevated serum ACE, hypercalcaemia.
Q49

A Patient with history of shortness of breath has Decreased FEV1/FVC Ratio, Normal DLCO. A 200 ml increase in baseline FEV1 is observed 15 minutes after administration of bronchodilators. The likely diagnosis

Löfgren's syndrome: BHL + erythema nodosum + arthritis = good prognosis sarcoidosis.
Q50

Bronchial Asthma is best diagnosed by:

Heerfordt's syndrome: sarcoidosis with uveitis + parotid enlargement + CN VII palsy.
Q51

Anti-inflammatory action on airways is seen with (Mark two correct option):

IPF: honeycombing (subpleural), bilateral basal crackles, restrictive pattern. Poor prognosis.
Q52

All of the following are used in Bronchial Asthma, Except:

IPF treatment: nintedanib or pirfenidone (antifibrotics). No cure except lung transplant.
Q53

All of the following drugs useful in the treatment of a patient with acute bronchial asthma except : (AIIMS Nov 03)

UIP: histological pattern of IPF.
Q54

Leukotriene inhibitors are used in asthma for -

HRCT of UIP/IPF: bilateral subpleural basal reticular pattern with honeycombing.
Q55

All of the following statements about Leukotriene modifiers in the management of bronchial asthma are true except :

Hypersensitivity pneumonitis: type III + type IV hypersensitivity to organic antigens.
Q56

Antibody used in the treatment of Bronchial Asthma is:

Farmer's lung: HP from Saccharopolyspora rectivirgula in mouldy hay.
Q57

The most predictive and dangerous side effect of propranolol that makes it to be avoided in known patient of COPD is induction of :

Bird fancier's lung: HP from avian proteins.
Q58

A drug is to be delivered by a nebuliser. The size of a droplet for its humidification is :

COP/BOOP: patchy consolidation, responds well to corticosteroids.
Q59

All of the following are used to characterize Asthma, Except :

Pulmonary alveolar proteinosis: 'crazy paving' on HRCT. Whole lung lavage is treatment.
Q60

Increased Reid Index is used to characterized:

Goodpasture's: anti-GBM antibodies → pulmonary haemorrhage + crescentic GN.
Q61

Chronic bronchitis is said to be present when patient has chronic cough:

Idiopathic pulmonary haemosiderosis: repeated pulmonary haemorrhages, no renal involvement.
Q62

All of the following are true about Chronic Bronchitis, Except:

Lung cancer: adenocarcinoma most common overall and in non-smokers.
Q63

All of the following are clinical signs of COPD except:

SCLC: most aggressive, early metastasis, Kulchitsky cells, paraneoplastic syndromes.
Q64

Which of the following is the least common bacteria responsible for Acute Exacerbation of Chronic Bronchitis

Most common lung cancer in non-smokers: Adenocarcinoma.
Q65

α₁ Antitrypsin deficiency is associated with :

Pancoast tumour: apical lung cancer → Horner's syndrome + arm pain + rib erosion.
Q66

All of the following statements about Emphysema are true, Except:

Squamous cell carcinoma: central airways, smoking, PTHrP → hypercalcaemia.
Q67

Lung functions in Emphysema reveal all of the following , Except

SCLC paraneoplastic: SIADH, Eaton-Lambert syndrome, ectopic ACTH/Cushing's.
Q68

All of the following are true about Emphysema, except:

Lambert-Eaton vs MG: Lambert-Eaton = strength IMPROVES with repeated activity.
Q69

Hoover’s Sign is most characteristic of

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

SVCO: most common cause is lung cancer (SCLC).
Q70

Gold criteria for Very Severe COPD is best represented by

Pemberton's sign: arm elevation → facial congestion in SVCO.
Q71

GOLD staging to assess severity is used for

Solitary pulmonary nodule: benign features — smooth, calcified, no growth. Malignant: spiculated, growing.
Q72

In an emphysematous patient with bullous lesions, which is the best investigation to measure lung volume:

Fleischner Society guidelines for SPN follow-up.
Q73

In a patient with COPD, Which of the following is most closely associated with decreased mortality

Light's criteria for exudate: protein ratio >0.5, LDH ratio >0.6.
Q74

First line therapy for management of COPD is

Most common exudative pleural effusion: malignancy (developed countries), TB (developing).
Q75

Lung volume reduction surgery is recommended for patients with all of the following, except

Most common transudative pleural effusion: congestive heart failure.
Q76

Bilateral upper lobe bronchiectasis suggests a diagnosis of:

Mesothelioma: associated with asbestosis. Very poor prognosis.
Q77

Bronchiectasis is most common in which lobe:

Asbestosis: interstitial fibrosis + pleural plaques. Lower lobe predominance.
Q78

Bronchiectasis Sicca is seen with:

Silicosis: upper lobe fibrosis, eggshell calcification of hilar nodes.
Q79

Brock’s Syndrome is:

CWP: Caplan's syndrome = CWP + rheumatoid arthritis + large pulmonary nodules.
Q80

Which of the following is NOT a complication of bronchiectasis : (AI 1998)

Berylliosis: similar to sarcoidosis. BeLPT diagnostic.
Q81

Which of the following is presently the most common cause of death in a patient with chronic extensive bronchiectasis

Pulmonary oedema: bat-wing pattern, Kerley B lines, cardiomegaly in cardiogenic PE.
Q82

Imaging modality for confirming the diagnosis of bronchiectasis is:

ARDS: bilateral infiltrates, PaO2/FiO2 <300.
Q83

Rampal, 45 yr old man presents with history of recurrent hemoptysis and purulent sputum. His chest X- Ray is normal, which of the following will be the next best investigation for him?

ARDS: lung-protective ventilation (6 mL/kg IBW), plateau pressure ≤30 cmH2O, prone positioning for severe.
Q84

All of the following are CT findings in Bronchiectasis, except

Tension pneumothorax: tracheal deviation + haemodynamic instability → emergency needle decompression.
Q85

A 1½ months old child with cough, mild stridor is started on oral antibiotics. The child showed initial improvement but later developed wheeze, productive cough and mild fever. X-ray shows hyperlucency and PFT shows an obstructive curve. The most probable diagnosis is:

Tension pneumothorax: needle decompression at 2nd ICS MCL, then chest tube 5th ICS MAL.
Q86

All are true about Interstitial Fibrosis, Except

OSA: AHI ≥5/hr. Loud snoring, daytime sleepiness, morning headache.
Q87

All of the following are true about interstitial lung disease, Except:

OSA treatment: CPAP is gold standard.
Q88

A 40 year old female presents with progressive dyspnea for one year. Physical examination reveals bibasilar end-inspiratory crepitations. All of the following statements about her condition are true, except:

Pickwickian syndrome: BMI >30 + PaCO2 >45 + OSA.
Q89

A female presents with history of progressive breathlessness. Histology shows heterogenous patchy fibrosis with several fibroblastic foci. The most likely diagnosis is:

Central sleep apnea: absent respiratory effort. Associated with CHF, stroke, opioids.
Q90

Investigation of choice for interstitial disease is:

Cheyne-Stokes breathing: cyclical waxing-waning tidal volume. CHF, stroke.
Q91

Investigation of choice for detection and characterization of interstitial lung disease is:

Biot's breathing: irregular, unpredictable. Severe CNS/medullary disease.
Q92

Farmer's Lung is caused due to exposure to:

Kussmaul breathing: deep regular breathing. Metabolic acidosis (DKA, uraemia).
Q94

Occupational Lung Disease commonly seen in Textile Industry Workers is:

CURB-65: Confusion, Urea, RR, BP, age ≥65. Score ≥3 = severe CAP.
Q95

Which of the following is the most common initial symptom of byssinosis?

CURB-65: 0-1 = outpatient, 2 = short admission, ≥3 = severe/consider ICU.
Q96

Caplan Syndrome is Pneumoconiosis with

HAP: ≥48 hours after admission. Gram-negatives and MRSA common.
Q97

All of the following features are seen in asbestosis except-

VAP prevention: head-of-bed elevation 30-45°, oral care, subglottic suctioning.
Q98

True statements about asbestosis –

Aspiration pneumonia: right lower lobe (supine) or right middle lobe (upright).
Q99

All of the following statements about silicosis are true, Except

Lung abscess: air-fluid level on CXR. Anaerobes most common. Treat: metronidazole + penicillin.
Q100

Which of the following is true about pneumoconiosis

Most common site of lung abscess: posterior segment of right upper lobe.
Q101

Not seen idiopathic pulmonary hemosiderosis

Haemoptysis: most common cause worldwide: TB.
Q102

All of the following are true about Idiopathic Pulmonary Hemosiderosis Except:

Massive haemoptysis: bronchial artery embolisation first-line. Surgery if failed.
Q103

All of the following are associated with pulmonary eosinophilic pneumonia, Except:

Clubbing in lung disease: lung cancer, bronchiectasis, abscess, empyema, mesothelioma, IPF.
Q104

Lower lobe fibrosis is seen in :

HPOA: periosteal new bone formation, clubbing. Associated with lung cancer (squamous cell).
Q105

All of the following constitute primary diagnostic criteria for ABPA, EXCEPT:

Chylothorax: milky fluid, high triglycerides. Causes: thoracic duct injury, lymphoma.
Q106

Diagnostic features of allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA) include all of the following except:

Peak flow meter: monitoring asthma control. Diurnal variation >20% = significant.
Q107

Diagnostic criteria for Allergic Bronchopulmonary Aspergillosis include all, except

Spirometry: obstructive = ↓FEV1/FVC. Restrictive = ↓FVC, normal/↑FEV1/FVC.
Q108

A 40 year old man presented with repeated episodes of bronchospasm and hemoptysis. Chest X-ray revealed perihilar bronchiectasis. The most likely diagnosis is :

DLCO: reduced in emphysema, IPF, pulmonary vascular disease.
Q109

All the following are true about bronchopulmonary aspergillosis except:

Flow-volume loop: obstructive = concave expiratory limb. Fixed upper airway = plateau both limbs.
Q110

True statements about ABPA include all of the following, Except:

COPD PFT: ↓FEV1, ↓FEV1/FVC, ↑TLC, ↑RV.
Q111

Which is not true about aspergillosis-

HP: mixed restrictive-obstructive pattern on PFT. DLCO reduced.
