Neurology

471 Questions

▼
0/471 answered 0 correct
Q1

A 45-year-old man presents with a daily headache. He describes two attacks per day over the past 3 weeks. Each attack lasts about an hour and awakens the patient from sleep. The patient has noted associated tearing and reddening of his right eye as well as nasal stuffiness. The pain is deep, excruciating, and limited to the right side of the head. The neurologic examination is nonfocal. The most likely diagnosis of this patient's headache is:

Lacunar infarcts: small deep infarcts in basal ganglia/thalamus/pons. Due to lipohyalinosis. RF: hypertension.
Q2

Cluster headache is characterized by all, except:

MCA stroke: contralateral hemiplegia + hemianesthesia (face>arm>leg), hemianopia, aphasia (dominant).
Q3

A female has episodic, recurrent headache in left hemicranium with nausea and paresthesia on right upper and lower limbs is most probably suffering from:

PCA stroke: contralateral homonymous hemianopia with macular sparing.
Q4

A female aged 30, presents with episodic throbbing headache for past 4 yrs. It usually involves one half of the face and is associated with nausea and vomiting. There is no aura. Most likely diagnosis is:

Weber's syndrome: ipsilateral CN III palsy + contralateral hemiplegia. Midbrain lesion.
Q5

A woman complains of headache associated with paresthesias of the right upper and lower limb: likely diagnosis is:

Wallenberg's syndrome: ipsilateral facial loss + contralateral body loss, ipsilateral Horner's, dysphagia.
Q6

Ophthalmoplegic migraine means:

Millard-Gubler: ipsilateral CN VI + VII palsy + contralateral hemiplegia. Pons.
Q7

A woman has bilateral headache that worsens with emotional stress; she has two children, both doing badly in school; diagnosis is:

INO (MLF lesion): ipsilateral adduction failure + contralateral abducting nystagmus. Bilateral INO = MS.
Q8

A 64 year old lady Kamla complains of severe unilateral headache on the right side and blindness for 2 days. On examination there is a thick cord like structure on the lateral side of the head. The ESR is 80 mm/hr in the first hour. The most likely diagnosis is:

Horner's syndrome: ptosis + miosis + anhidrosis. Pancoast tumour, carotid dissection.
Q9

A 35 year old Lady Malti has unilateral headache, nausea, vomiting and visual blurring. The diagnosis is:

Argyll Robertson pupil: accommodates but does NOT react to light. Classic for neurosyphilis.
Q10

A young girl presents with repeated episodes of throbbing occipital headache associated with ataxia and vertigo. The family history is positive for similar headaches in her mother. Most likely diagnosis is:

CN III palsy: dilated pupil, ptosis, 'down and out' eye. Painful III nerve palsy = PCoA aneurysm.
Q11

All of the following statements about treatment of migraine are true, Except:

Bacterial meningitis CSF: cloudy, ↑↑PMNs, ↑↑protein, ↓↓glucose.
Q12

What is drug of choice for acute attack of migraine:

TB meningitis CSF: clear/xanthochromic, ↑lymphocytes, ↑protein, ↓glucose, cobweb clot.
Q13

All of the following agents are used for prophylaxis of migraine, Except:

Viral meningitis CSF: clear, ↑lymphocytes, normal/slightly ↑protein, normal glucose.
Q14

A 52 years old male patient presented with multiple episodes of unilateral short accompanying conjunctival congestion headache lacrimation. Which of the following is the diagnosis of this patient?

Bacterial meningitis Rx: ceftriaxone + dexamethasone. Add ampicillin for Listeria.
Q15

UMN lesion is characterised by:

Most common bacterial meningitis in adults: Streptococcus pneumoniae.
Q16

Which of the following is NOT a sign of upper motor neuron paralysis:

Herpes encephalitis: HSV-1, temporal lobe. Treat with IV acyclovir.
Q17

With which one of the following Lower motor neuron lesions are associated?

Cryptococcal meningitis: AIDS (CD4 <100). India ink prep. Treat: amphotericin B + flucytosine.
Q18

Fasciculations are seen in:

Brain abscess: ring-enhancing lesion on CT. Most common organism: Streptococcus milleri.
Q19

Fasciculation is seen in:

Most common ring-enhancing brain lesion in AIDS: Toxoplasma gondii.
Q21

Involvement of pyramidal tract leads to all of the following except:

Raised ICP: papilloedema, Cushing's triad (HTN + bradycardia + irregular breathing).
Q22

Which of the following is a feature of Extrapyramidal Tract Involvement:

Normal CSF opening pressure: 70-180 mm H2O.
Q23

Distribution of weakness in Pyramidal tract lesions:

SAH: thunderclap headache. CT first (90% sensitive within 6 hours), then LP (xanthochromia).
Q24

Bulbar paralysis refers to:

SAH: most common cause = ruptured berry aneurysm (80%). Most common site: AComA.
Q25

All of the following are features of Pseudobulbar palsy, except:

Subdural haematoma: crescent-shaped hyperdensity. Bridging vein rupture.
Q26

Which among the following does not cause exaggerated jaw jerk:

Epidural haematoma: biconvex lens-shaped. Middle meningeal artery rupture. Lucid interval.
Q27

All are features of pyramidal tract lesion, Except:

Epilepsy first-line: tonic-clonic → sodium valproate or levetiracetam. Absence → ethosuximide.
Q28

Which of the following represents the site of lesion in Motor Neuron disease:

Temporal lobe epilepsy: déjà vu, olfactory hallucinations, automatisms. MRI: hippocampal sclerosis.
Q29

Which is pathognomonic for motor neuron disease:

Infantile spasms (West syndrome): flexion spasms + hypsarrhythmia. Treat: ACTH or vigabatrin.
Q30

Spinomuscular atrophy is seen in lesion of:

Status epilepticus: IV lorazepam → IV fosphenytoin/valproate → general anaesthesia.
Q31

Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis involve:

MS: periventricular plaques, Dawson's fingers on MRI. CSF: oligoclonal bands.
Q32

Hexanucleotide repeats are seen in:

Uhthoff's phenomenon: worsening of MS symptoms with heat.
Q33

A middle aged man presents with progressive atrophy and weakness of hands & forearms. On examination he is found to have slight spasticity of the legs, generalized hyper-reflexia and increased signal in the cortico-spinal tracts on T2 weighted MRI. The most likely diagnosis is:

MS treatment: interferon-beta, glatiramer (first-line). Natalizumab, fingolimod (second-line).
Q34

Motor neuron disease, TRUE is:

GBS: ascending flaccid paralysis, areflexia, albuminocytological dissociation.
Q35

Fasciculations are seen in:

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

GBS most common preceding infection: Campylobacter jejuni.
Q36

Which of the following sensation are transmitted by the Dorsal Tract/ Posterior column:

GBS treatment: IVIG or plasmapheresis. Steroids NOT effective.
Q37

Which of the following is not affected by a lesion in posterior column of spinal cord:

Miller Fisher syndrome: ophthalmoplegia + ataxia + areflexia. Anti-GQ1b antibodies.
Q38

Which is not affected in lesion of posterior column of spinal cord:

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

MG: AChR antibodies in 85%. Fatigable weakness, ocular symptoms.
Q39

A ventrolateral cordotomy is performed to produce relief of pain from the right leg. It is effective because it interrupts the:

MG diagnosis: Tensilon test, AChR antibodies, repetitive nerve stimulation (decremental response).
Q40

Type of sensation lost on same side in Brown sequard syndrome is:

MG treatment: pyridostigmine, thymectomy, steroids. IVIG or plasmapheresis for crisis.
Q41

Which of the following is true about Brown Sequard syndrome?

Cholinergic crisis: SLUDGE + miosis + bradycardia + weakness.
Q42

All of the following are true about Brown Sequard Syndrome, except:

Lambert-Eaton: strength IMPROVES with repeated activity (opposite to MG). Anti-VGCC Ab. SCLC.
Q43

The following are components of Brown Sequard syndrome except:

PD: resting 'pill-rolling' tremor, rigidity (cogwheel), bradykinesia, postural instability. TRAP.
Q44

Which of the following statements about Brown Sequard Syndrome is true:

Lewy bodies: alpha-synuclein + ubiquitin inclusions in substantia nigra neurons.
Q45

Which of the following is not a feature of extramedullary tumour:

PD treatment: levodopa + carbidopa (gold standard). Dopamine agonists, MAO-B inhibitors.
Q46

Clinical features of Conus Medullaris syndrome include all of the following Except:

Levodopa side effects: on-off phenomena, dyskinesias, psychosis, orthostatic hypotension.
Q47

Clinical Features of conus medullaris include all of the following except:

DBS: subthalamic nucleus (STN) most common target for advanced PD.
Q48

Early loss of bladder control is seen in:

PSP: vertical gaze palsy (downgaze first) + axial rigidity + frequent falls backward. Tau.
Q49

Hypotension in Acute Spinal Cord Injury is due to:

Huntington's: CAG repeat (chromosome 4), caudate atrophy, chorea + dementia + psychiatric. AD.
Q50

Spinal shock is characterized by (select three best options):

Wilson's disease: AR, ATP7B gene, copper → liver + brain + eyes. Low ceruloplasmin.
Q51

Spinal shock is characterized by all of the following, Except:

KF rings: greenish-brown deposits in cornea in Wilson's disease.
Q52

Cervical cord injury does not cause:

Wilson's treatment: D-penicillamine (first-line) or trientine.
Q53

An unconscious patient is brought to the Emergency Department with suspected cervical cord injury. Which of the following clinical signs is not suggestive of cervical spinal cord injury.

Hemiballismus: large-amplitude flinging movements. STN lesion (contralateral).
Q54

Which of the following signs is not suggestive of a cervical spinal cord injury:

Cerebellar ataxia: ipsilateral limb ataxia, dysdiadochokinesia, intention tremor.
Q55

Spastic paraplegia is caused by all, except:

Friedreich's ataxia: AR, GAA repeat in frataxin gene. Pes cavus, hypertrophic CMP.
Q56

Causes of Acute Flaccid Paralysis include:

Brown-Séquard: ipsilateral UMN weakness + proprioception/vibration loss + contralateral pain/temperature loss.
Q57

A patient involved in a road traffic accident presents with quadriparesis, sphincter disturbance, sensory level up to the upper border of sternum and a respiratory rate of 35/minute. The likely level of lesion is:

Anterior cord: bilateral weakness + pain/temperature loss. Proprioception PRESERVED.
Q58

A patient with traumatic paraplegia due to injury of the thoracic cord of 'T level' is observed to have a blood pressure of 210/120. What should be the initial management?

Central cord: most common incomplete SCI. Weakness arm > leg.
Q59

Beevor's Sign is seen in:

Cauda equina: flaccid weakness, saddle anaesthesia, bowel/bladder dysfunction. Emergency surgery.
Q60

Beevor's sign indicates a lesion in the:

Syringomyelia: cape-like dissociated sensory loss, painless burns/injuries.
Q61

Anterior spinal Artery thrombosis is characterized by all, except:

SCD (B12 deficiency): posterior column + lateral corticospinal tract + peripheral nerve degeneration.
Q62

First sign of spinal cord compression in TB Spine is:

Tabes dorsalis: posterior column degeneration, Argyll Robertson pupil, lightning pains, Charcot joints.
Q63

The first clinical manifestation of Spinal Tuberculosis is:

ALS: UMN + LMN signs. SOD1 mutation in familial ALS. Riluzole is only FDA-approved drug.
Q64

Most common drug used in spasticity in patients with spinal cord injury is:

Diabetic neuropathy: most common cause of peripheral neuropathy worldwide.
Q65

A 12-year-old boy presents to the Outpatient department with history of progressively increasing difficulty in walking and frequent falls. Physical examination reveals an ataxic gait and nystagmus. All deep tendon reflexes were observed to be absent while the plantar response was 'Extensor'. What is the most likely diagnosis?

GBS CSF: high protein (100-1000 mg/dL), normal/minimal cells.
Q66

All of the following are features of Friedreich's Ataxia, Except:

CMT disease (CMT1A): PMP22 duplication. Foot drop, pes cavus, 'inverted champagne bottle' legs.
Q67

Bilateral loss of ankle jerk and extensor plantar response is seen in:

Carpal tunnel: median nerve at wrist. Tinel's and Phalen's signs positive.
Q68

Features of Friedreich's Ataxia include all of the following except:

Central CN VII palsy: forehead SPARED. Peripheral (Bell's) palsy: entire face including forehead.
Q69

Pyramidal tract involvement with absent ankle jerk is seen in:

Trigeminal neuralgia: lancinating unilateral facial pain. Carbamazepine first-line.
Q70

Absent ankle jerk and extensor plantar response is found in:

Migraine: unilateral throbbing, nausea, photophobia. Triptans (5-HT1B/1D agonists) for acute.
Q71

Which of the following regions of the spinal cord is involved first in Friedreich's Ataxia:

Cluster headache: unilateral periorbital pain + autonomic features. 100% O2 for acute attack.
Q72

Earliest presentation of Friedreich's ataxia is:

Tension headache: bilateral pressing, mild-moderate. Most common headache.
Q73

Vitamin B12 deficiency can give rise to all of the following, except:

IIH: obese young women, headache, papilloedema, raised CSF pressure, normal CT. Acetazolamide.
Q74

Subacute Combined Degeneration of Cord is caused due to deficiency of?

NPH: wet + wobbly + wacky. Normal CSF pressure. Responds to shunting.
Q75

Subacute combined degeneration of spinal cord is associated with deficiency of which of the following Vitamins?

Alzheimer's: senile plaques (beta-amyloid) + neurofibrillary tangles (tau). Hippocampal atrophy.
Q76

A 65-year-old man presents with anemia, posterior column dysfunction, and Plantar Extensor. Which of the following is the likely cause?

ApoE4: major genetic risk factor for sporadic Alzheimer's disease.
Q77

65 year old man presents with anaemia and posterior column dysfunction, the likely cause is:

FTD: personality/behaviour changes, language impairment. TDP-43 or tau pathology.
Q78

Subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord is a sequel of which of the following?

DLB: dementia + fluctuating cognition + visual hallucinations + parkinsonism. Avoid antipsychotics.
Q79

All are true about subacute combined degeneration of spinal cord except:

CJD: rapidly progressive dementia + myoclonus + periodic sharp waves on EEG. Prion disease.
Q80

Which of the following is not true about Tabes Dorsalis:

Neurocysticercosis: most common cause of acquired epilepsy in developing countries.
Q81

A patient known case of syphilis presented with gait disturbances and frequent falls in dark. On examination, he is found to have a high steppage gait. Which Tract is typically involved in this condition?

NF1: chromosome 17. Café-au-lait spots, neurofibromas, Lisch nodules.
Q82

Subacute combined degeneration due to Vit B12 deficiency mainly involves:

NF2: bilateral acoustic neuromas. Chromosome 22.
Q83

All are features of tabes dorsalis, except:

Tuberous sclerosis: chromosome 9 (TSC1/hamartin) or 16 (TSC2/tuberin). Ash-leaf spots, shagreen patches, adenoma sebaceum (facial angiofibromas), cardiac rhabdomyoma.
Q84

Features of Syringomyelia include all of the following, except:

Von Hippel-Lindau: VHL gene. Cerebellar haemangioblastoma, renal cell carcinoma, retinal angiomas.
Q85

Which of the following sensory loss occurs in syringomyelia:

Sturge-Weber: port-wine stain (trigeminal territory) + leptomeningeal angioma + epilepsy. Tram-track calcifications on skull X-ray.
Q86

Painless burn in hand is a characteristic feature of:

Wernicke's encephalopathy: thiamine (B1) deficiency. Triad: ophthalmoplegia + ataxia + confusion. Treat with IV thiamine BEFORE glucose.
Q87

Cerebral ischemia occurs when cerebral blood flow is less than:

Korsakoff's psychosis: chronic thiamine deficiency. Anterograde amnesia + confabulation + retrograde amnesia. Mammillary body atrophy.
Q88

Most common cause of cerebrovascular accident is:

Posterior fossa tumours in children: medulloblastoma (most common), cerebellar astrocytoma, ependymoma, brain stem glioma.
Q89

Commonest cause of cerebrovascular accident is:

Medulloblastoma: most common brain tumour in children. PNET. Radiates to CSF early. Treat: surgery + radiotherapy + chemotherapy.
Q90

Most common cause of embolic stroke is?

Glioblastoma multiforme (GBM): most common primary brain tumour in adults. Poor prognosis. 'Butterfly glioma' across corpus callosum.
Q91

Most common cause of stroke in young women in India among OCP users:

Meningioma: second most common primary brain tumour in adults. From arachnoid cap cells. Usually benign, slowly growing. Psammoma bodies.
Q92

Most common cause of intracranial haemorrhage is:

Acoustic neuroma (vestibular schwannoma): unilateral sensorineural hearing loss, tinnitus, vertigo. CN VIII.
Q93

The commonest cause of Intracerebral Bleed is:

Pituitary adenoma: most common intracranial tumour overall. Prolactinoma most common type. Bitemporal hemianopia (suprasellar extension).
Q95

The most common location of hypertensive intracranial hemorrhage is:

Craniopharyngioma: Rathke's pouch remnant. Children and elderly. Bitemporal hemianopia, calcification on imaging (suprasellar).
Q96

Which of the following is the most common location of hypertensive hemorrhage?

Most common brain metastasis: lung cancer (especially SCLC). Then breast, melanoma, renal, colon.
Q97

The most common site for hypertensive bleed is?

Essential tremor: 6-12 Hz action/postural tremor. Improves with alcohol. Beta-blockers (propranolol) or primidone for treatment.
Q98

Which of the following is the most common location of hypertensive hemorrhage?

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Dystonia: sustained muscle contractions causing abnormal postures. Focal (most common in adults: writer's cramp, torticollis). Treat with botulinum toxin.
Q100

The most common intracranial site of hypertensive hemorrhage is:

Myoclonus: brief, sudden, involuntary muscle jerks. Physiological (sleep jerks), essential, epileptic, symptomatic. Treat underlying cause.
Q101

The common cause of subarachnoid hemorrhage is:

Tics: sudden repetitive stereotyped movements. Tourette syndrome: motor + vocal tics >1 year.
Q102

Which of the following is the MOST frequent cause of spontaneous subarachnoid hemorrhage?

Restless legs syndrome: urge to move legs, worse at rest and at night, relieved by movement. Treat: dopamine agonists (pramipexole), iron if deficient.
Q103

The most common cause of subarachnoid hemorrhage is:

Bell's palsy: idiopathic peripheral CN VII palsy. HSV-1 association. Treat: prednisolone ± acyclovir within 72 hours.
Q104

Most common cause of subarachnoid hemorrhage is:

Pseudobulbar palsy: UMN lesion bilateral corticobulbar tracts. Donald Duck speech, emotional lability, brisk jaw jerk, bilateral pyramidal signs.
Q105

Commonest cause of subarachnoid haemorrhage is:

Bulbar palsy: LMN lesion of CN IX, X, XI, XII. Nasal speech, dysphagia, absent gag reflex.
Q106

Berry aneurysm is caused by:

Narcolepsy: EDS, cataplexy (loss of muscle tone triggered by emotion), sleep paralysis, hypnagogic hallucinations. Low CSF hypocretin-1.
Q107

Which is least common site of berry aneurysm:

Peripheral vertigo vs central vertigo: peripheral = horizontal/rotatory nystagmus with fatigability, inhibited by fixation. Central = vertical/direction-changing nystagmus, not inhibited by fixation.
Q108

The most common site of Berry aneurysm is:

BPPV: most common cause of peripheral vertigo. Dix-Hallpike test positive. Treat: Epley manoeuvre.
Q109

Most common site for berry aneurysm rupture?

Menière's disease: episodic vertigo + tinnitus + fluctuating sensorineural hearing loss + aural fullness. Endolymphatic hydrops.
Q110

Rupture of berry aneurysm most commonly results in:

Acoustic neuroma: unilateral progressive sensorineural hearing loss + tinnitus + vertigo. MRI with gadolinium.
Q111

A patient with suspected subarachnoid haemorrhage presents with blood isolated in the fourth ventricle on a CT scan. Aneurysmal rupture is likely to have resulted from:

Spinal cord levels: C5 = shoulder abduction; C6 = elbow flexion; C7 = elbow extension; C8/T1 = hand intrinsics; L4 = knee extension; L5 = ankle dorsiflexion; S1 = ankle plantarflexion.
Q112

Which of the following is the most common cause of late neurological deterioration in a case of cerebrovascular accident:

Horner's syndrome levels: Central (hypothalamus to ciliospinal centre of Budge) vs preganglionic (T1 root to stellate ganglion) vs postganglionic (superior cervical ganglion to eye).
Q113

A patient known to have mitral stenosis and atrial fibrillation presents with acute onset of weakness in the left upper limb which recovered completely in two weeks. The most likely diagnosis is:

Tensilon (edrophonium) test: short-acting anticholinesterase. Positive (improvement in ptosis/diplopia) confirms myasthenia gravis.
Q114

After a transient ischemic attack chances of stroke is maximum in?

Electromyography (EMG): decremental response on repetitive nerve stimulation = MG. Incremental response = Lambert-Eaton syndrome.
Q115

All of the following statements about Ischemic Penumbra are true, except:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q116

Emboli in 'Hollenhorst plaque' are composed of:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q117

All of the following statements about Moya-Moya disease are true, except:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q118

'Duret Hemorrhages' are seen in:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q119

A 45 years old hypertensive male presented with sudden onset severe headache, vomiting and neck stiffness. On examination he didn't have any focal neurological deficit. His CT scan showed blood in the Sylvian fissure. The probable diagnosis is:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q120

A 45 year old male patient presented in the casualty with two hours history of sudden onset of severe headache associated with nausea and vomiting on clinical examination the patient had neck stiffness and right sided ptosis. Rest of the neurological examination was normal. What is the clinical diagnosis:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q121

A nondiabetic, non hypertensive adult male develops sudden severe headache with altered sensorium, likely diagnosis is:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q122

A young female presents with severe headache and neck stiffness of abrupt onset. She says, she has never had such severe headache before. She also complains of associated nausea and photophobia. Likely diagnosis is:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q123

Sudden excruciating headache is most characteristic of:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q124

A patient presented with thunder clap headache followed by unconsciousness and progressive III cranial nerve palsy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q125

A 25-year-old male had severe headache followed by unconsciousness. CSF tap revealed red blood cells. Most likely diagnosis is:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q126

Motor aphasia refers to defect in:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q127

Lesion in inferior frontal gyrus causes:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q128

All of the following are true about Broca's aphasia except:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q129

Broca's aphasia is?

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q130

Aphasia which affects the arcuate fibres is called?

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q131

A patient being evaluated for aphasia is unable to repeat sentences correctly or to name objects properly. However, the patient's speech is effortless and melodic. There are frequent errors in word choice and obvious difficulties in comprehension. The remainder of the patient's neurologic examination is normal. Damage in which area of the brain would account for this type of aphasia:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q132

All of the following are Fluent Aphasia's Except:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q133

Fluent Aphasia with preserved comprehension and impaired repetition is:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q134

Which of the following speech indicates damage to the categorical hemisphere:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q135

A lesion in the paracentral lobule causes:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q136

A lesion in the paracentral lobule causes:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q137

A patient with injury to paracentral lobule has all of the following features except?

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q138

All of the following are features of temporal lobe lesions Except:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q139

Lesions of the medial temporal lobe may produce (select three options):

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q140

In a Right handed person, left parietal lobe injury may be associated with all of the following except:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q141

In isolated Gerstmann's syndrome, the damage is in:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q142

Gerstmann's Syndrome includes all of the following except?

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q143

Paratonic rigidity or gegenhalten results from disease of:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q144

In a right handed person, left temporoparietal lobe injury leads to all, Except:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q145

Anterior cerebral artery occlusion can cause:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q146

Hemiplegia is commonly associated with infarction of the area of distribution of the:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q147

All of the following are true about anterior choroidal artery syndrome except:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q148

Which of the following is not a usual feature of right middle cerebral artery territory infarct:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q150

Which of the following sites is not involved in a posterior cerebral artery infarct:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q151

All of the following syndromes are caused by involvement of the posterior cerebral artery, except:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q152

What is the site of lesion in a patient with Alexia without agraphia?

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q153

Anton's syndrome results due to occlusion of?

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q154

In Anton's syndrome, patient is often unaware of:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q155

Memory impairment occurs in embolism of posterior cerebral artery because of damage to:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q156

A 45 years male presents with hypertension. He has sudden abnormal flinging movements in right upper and lower limbs. Most likely site of haemorrhage is:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q157

A hypertensive individual had a sudden headache and became unconscious within a few minutes. On regaining consciousness, there was complete flaccid hemiplegia with no involvement of upper face, absence of tendon reflexes and a positive Babinski sign. Which one of the following arteries could have ruptured:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q158

The site of lesion in Dejerine-Roussy syndrome is:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q159

All of the following Neurophysiological defects are likely to result from right lobe involvement, except:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q161

All of the following are features of Millard Gubler Syndrome, Except:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q162

Third nerve palsy with pupillary sparing is seen in all except:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q163

Which of the following structure is most likely to be compressed by an aneurysm of the posterior communicating artery:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q164

Most common nerve involved in intracranial aneurysm is:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q165

Which of the following artery is likely to be involved in 3rd cranial nerve lesion?

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q166

Triad of Balint's syndrome includes all of the following, except:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q167

Damage to Auditory Cortex on one side causes:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q168

Lacunar infarcts are caused by:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q169

Lacunar infarcts may manifest all of the following except:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q170

All of the following manifestations may be associated with lacunar infarcts, except:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q171

Which is true about carotid stenosis:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q172

Investigation of choice for screening of proximal internal carotid artery stenosis is:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q173

Lateral Medullary Syndrome is most frequently due to:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q174

Which of the following syndrome occurs due to blockage of PICA?

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q175

Lesion of posterior inferior cerebellar artery at brain involves/affects all of the following, except:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q176

Lateral Medullary Syndrome involves all of the following cranial nerves, Except:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q177

All are features of Wallenberg's syndrome, Except:

🫘

Nephrology

293 Questions

▼
0/293 answered 0 correct
Q1

Urinalysis shows RBC casts; likely source is:

Nephrotic syndrome: massive proteinuria (>3.5 g/day), hypoalbuminaemia, oedema, hyperlipidaemia.
Q2

Presence of which of the following in the urine is diagnostic of glomerular injury:

Most common nephrotic in adults: Membranous nephropathy. In children: Minimal change disease.
Q3

In hematuria of glomerular origin the urine is characterized by the presence of all of the following except:

MCD: most common nephrotic in children. Normal LM, foot process effacement EM. Responds to steroids.
Q4

Which of the following mucoproteins forms the matrix of a Hyline Cast

Membranous nephropathy: subepithelial deposits, PLA2R antibodies, 'spike and dome' on EM.
Q5

Recurrent gross hematuria is seen in al of the following Except

FSGS: most common nephrotic in Black adults in USA. Podocyte injury. Steroid-resistant.
Q6

Presence of which of the following correlates best with chronic renal failure

Nephritic syndrome: haematuria (RBC casts), proteinuria, hypertension, oliguria, AKI.
Q7

Broad casts seen in cases of -

PSGN: 1-3 weeks after streptococcal pharyngitis. 'Lumpy-bumpy' deposits. Low C3.
Q8

A boy is suffering from acute pyelonephritis. Most specific urinary finding will be-

IgA nephropathy: most common primary GN worldwide. Recurrent macroscopic haematuria after URTI.
Q9

Acute pyelonephritis and uncomplicated UTI may be differentiated by all of the following Except :

Alport syndrome: X-linked, COL4A5, hereditary nephritis, deafness, ocular abnormalities.
Q10

In differentiating Glomerular proteinuria from Tubular proteinuria, Glomerular proteinuria is indicated by:

Thin basement membrane disease: persistent microscopic haematuria. Normal renal function. Excellent prognosis.
Q11

Which of the following statements about orthostatic proteinuria is true:

Goodpasture's: anti-GBM antibodies → crescentic GN + pulmonary haemorrhage. Linear IgG on IF.
Q12

Urine dipstick shows result +3. What's the value protein appearing in mg/dl in urine is-

RPGN: Type I = anti-GBM; Type II = immune complex; Type III = pauci-immune (ANCA).
Q13

Bence Jones proteins are derived from:

GPA: c-ANCA/PR3. MPA: p-ANCA/MPO. EGPA: p-ANCA/MPO + eosinophilia.
Q14

Bence Jones proteinuria may be seen in:

Lupus nephritis class III/IV: most severe. 'Wire-loop' lesions. Treat with cyclophosphamide or MMF.
Q15

Which of the following screening tests suggests Bacteriuria

Diabetic nephropathy: Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules. Microalbuminuria is first detectable abnormality.
Q16

Leucocyte esterase dipstick test is done for

Microalbuminuria: 30-300 mg/day. Earliest sign of diabetic nephropathy. ACE-I/ARB slow progression.
Q17

In a child, non-functioning kidney is best diagnosed by

Hypertensive nephrosclerosis: arteriolar sclerosis ('onion skin'), tubular atrophy.
Q18

A patient presents with acute renal failure with normal ultrasound abdomen report. Next most useful investigation is :

AKI: creatinine rise ≥0.3 mg/dL in 48 hours OR ≥1.5× baseline in 7 days.
Q19

In urinary system disease, GI symptoms appears because of :

Prerenal AKI: BUN:Cr >20:1, FeNa <1%, hyaline casts.
Q20

Which of the following best methods of urine collection is associated with least contamination : *(AI

Intrinsic AKI (ATN): BUN:Cr 10-15:1, FeNa >2%, granular 'muddy brown' casts.
Q21

Most common agent causing catheter induced urinary tract infection is:

FeNa <1% = prerenal; >2% = intrinsic renal. Unreliable on diuretics.
Q22

All of the following are accepted guidelines for defining Catheter Associated UTI (CAUTI), Except

Hepatorenal syndrome: functional renal failure in cirrhosis. FeNa <1%, no proteinuria.
Q23

A patient in an inpatient unit has a Foley catheter inserted and the following day he is observed to have fever with suprapubic tenderness. Urine culture grows single organism with ≥10⁵ CFU/ml.His UTI can be best defined as

Rhabdomyolysis: myoglobin → ATN. Elevated CK, positive 'blood' urine with no RBCs on microscopy.
Q24

Definition of complicated urinary tract infection is, the infection which fail to resolve or recur within ___ week/s of standard therapy.

Contrast nephropathy prevention: IV normal saline, NAC, minimize contrast volume.
Q25

Coloured urine is not seen in :

CKD: GFR <60 mL/min for ≥3 months. KDIGO GFR categories G1-G5.
Q26

Anuria in clinical practice is defined as:

CKD complications: anaemia (↓EPO), secondary HPT (↓1,25-OH-D, ↑PTH), metabolic acidosis, hyperkalaemia.
Q27

Oliguria is defined as

Renal osteodystrophy: osteomalacia + osteitis fibrosa cystica. Treat with phosphate binders, calcitriol.
Q28

Polyuria is defined as urine output more than:

Dialysis indications: AEIOUs — Acidosis, Electrolytes (hyperkalaemia), Intoxication, Overload, Uraemia.
Q29

Which of the following best defines Microscopic Hematuria as:

Peritoneal dialysis: continuous, home-based, better haemodynamic stability.
Q30

Chyluria is associated with passage of urine which is:

Renal transplant: best RRT. Calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus/cyclosporine) for immunosuppression.
Q31

The most common cause of Chyluria is:

ADPKD: PKD1 (Chr 16, 85%) or PKD2 (Chr 4, 15%). Berry aneurysms, liver cysts, MVP.
Q32

Milky urine is most commonly observed with which of the following crystals

ADPKD: most common hereditary kidney disease. ACE-I for BP control.
Q33

Which of the following Urine crystals are rectangular prisms and resemble coffin lids:

ARPKD: neonates, bilateral flank masses, oligohydramnios, Potter sequence, hepatic fibrosis.
Q34

Creatinine Clearance test is used to assess:

Most common renal stone: calcium oxalate (75-80%). Radiopaque.
Q35

Which of the following is the most accurate method to assess decline in GFR during initial stages of Renal insufficiency

Calcium oxalate stones: hypercalciuria, hyperoxaluria, hypocitraturia. Treat: fluids, thiazides, K-citrate.
Q36

Which of the following statements about creatinine clearance test is true:

Uric acid stones: radiolucent on plain X-ray. Alkalinisation dissolves them.
Q37

Water Deprivation Test is used to assess

Cystinuria: AR, hexagonal crystals. Treat: hydration, alkalinisation, D-penicillamine.
Q38

Most unlikely cause of acute tubular necrosis amongst the following is:

RTA Type I (distal): urine pH >5.5, hypokalaemia, nephrocalcinosis.
Q39

The Diuretic Phase of Acute Renal Failure is typically begins after:

Fanconi syndrome: generalised proximal tubular dysfunction. Wilson's, cystinosis, myeloma.
Q40

Uremic complications typically arise during which of the following phases of Renal Failure:

Bartter syndrome: AR, NKCC2 defect. Hypokalaemia + metabolic alkalosis + hypercalciuria + normal BP.
Q41

Continuous Renal Replacement Therapy is initiated in which of the following phases of Acute Renal Failure:

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Gitelman syndrome: similar to Bartter's but magnesium wasting, hypocalciuria. Milder.
Q42

Which of the following values are suggestive of acute tubular necrosis :

Liddle syndrome: gain-of-function ENaC. HTN + hypokalaemia + metabolic alkalosis. Low renin, low aldosterone. Treat: amiloride.
Q43

Pre-renal azotemia is associated with one of the following characteristic feature :

Nephrogenic DI: resistance to ADH. Causes: lithium (most common drug), hypercalcaemia, hypokalaemia.
Q44

Pre-Renal Azotemia is characterized by all of the following. Except :

RCC: clear cell type most common (75%). Paraneoplastic: polycythaemia, hypercalcaemia, Stauffer syndrome.
Q45

Plasma urea / creatinine ratio of 20:1 may be seen in:

RCC paraneoplastic: polycythaemia (↑EPO), hypercalcaemia (PTHrP), Stauffer syndrome.
Q46

Fractional excretion of sodium <1 is seen in

Wilms' tumour: most common renal cancer in children. Associated with aniridia, WAGR syndrome.
Q47

Prerenal and renal azotemia is differentiated on the basis of:

TCC of renal pelvis/ureter: associated with smoking, analgesic nephropathy, cyclophosphamide.
Q48

In tubular necrosis, ratio of urine to plasma creatinine is:

UTI: E. coli most common (80%). S. saprophyticus in young sexually active women.
Q49

Which of the following is true about Acute Tubular Necrosis

Acute pyelonephritis: flank pain, fever, CVA tenderness, WBC casts in urine. Ciprofloxacin.
Q50

All of the following are true about Prerenal Azotemia, Except:

Vesicoureteral reflux: most common anatomical predisposing factor for recurrent UTI in children.
Q51

Which differentiating prerenal azotemia with ATN features favouring pre-renal azotemia (select two options):

Renal artery stenosis: atherosclerotic (older men) or fibromuscular dysplasia (young women).
Q52

All of the following are true about Oliguric ARF

Fibromuscular dysplasia: 'string of beads' on renal arteries. Treat with PTA.
Q53

Oliguric phase of renal failure, all are TRUE, EXCEPT:

TTP: ADAMTS13 deficiency. MAHA + thrombocytopenia + neurological + renal + fever.
Q54

Non Oliguric Acute Renal Failure is typically associated with

HUS: E. coli O157:H7 (Shiga toxin) in children. AKI + MAHA + thrombocytopenia. No neurological.
Q55

All of the following are seen in oliguric phase of Acute Tubular necrosis, Except:

Alport syndrome: X-linked, COL4A5. Males progress to ESRD. Sensorineural hearing loss, anterior lenticonus.
Q56

Hypophosphatemia is seen in all except :

Hyperkalaemia: peaked T waves → flat P waves → wide QRS → sine wave → VF. Treat: calcium gluconate, insulin+glucose, dialysis.
Q57

RIFLE criteria is used for diagnosis of-

Hyponatraemia: most common electrolyte disorder in hospitalised patients.
Q58

Feature of CRF include all, except:

SIADH: serum Na <135, osmolality <275, urine Na >40, urine osmolality >100 mOsm/kg.
Q59

Decrease in GFR is apparent in which of the following stages of Chronic Kidney Disease

SIADH treatment: fluid restriction. Tolvaptan (V2 antagonist) for severe/refractory.
Q60

Azotemia appears when residual GFR is:

Central pontine myelinolysis: complication of too-rapid correction of hyponatraemia. Correct ≤8-10 mEq/L per 24 hours.
Q61

Signs and symptoms of a Renal Failure first appear when Renal function deteriorates by more than:

Central DI: ADH deficiency → treat with DDAVP.

Gastroenterology

254 Questions

▼
0/254 answered 0 correct
Q1

(AI 2008) A 30-year-old female patient presents with non-progressive dysphagia for both solids and liquids. The characteristic finding on barium swallow that will confirm the probable diagnosis is:

GERD: LES incompetence. Barrett's oesophagus = metaplasia → risk of adenocarcinoma.
Q2

(AIIMS Nov 2003) Dysphagia lusoria is due to:

Barrett's: specialised intestinal metaplasia (goblet cells). Risk for oesophageal adenocarcinoma.
Q3

(AI 2009) Non-progressive contractions of the esophagus are:

Most common oesophageal cancer worldwide: squamous cell carcinoma.
Q4

A lady presented with progressive dysphagia only for solids. Barium study showed proximal esophageal dilatation with distal constriction. The most likely diagnosis is:

Achalasia: failure of LES relaxation + absent peristalsis. Bird's beak on barium. Treat: pneumatic dilatation or Heller myotomy.
Q5

(AIIMS May 2006) A 40-year-old female patient presents with dysphagia to both liquids and solids and regurgitation for 3 months. The dysphagia was non-progressive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

Diffuse oesophageal spasm: intermittent chest pain + dysphagia. 'Corkscrew' oesophagus on barium.
Q6

(GI June 2004) Intermittent dysphagia is caused by (select two best options):

PUD: H. pylori in >90% of duodenal ulcers.
Q7

Oesophageal motility disorders are best diagnosed by:

H. pylori diagnosis gold standard: urea breath test or stool antigen test.
Q8

(PGI Dec 2003) Investigation of choice for dysphagia for solids:

H. pylori triple therapy: PPI + amoxicillin + clarithromycin × 14 days.
Q9

A patient presents with dysphagia of 4 weeks duration. He is now able to swallow liquid food only. Which of the following is the one investigation to be done?

Zollinger-Ellison syndrome: gastrinoma → multiple severe peptic ulcers. MEN-1 association.
Q10

(PG June 2002) True about achalasia cardia (select two best options):

ZES diagnosis: fasting serum gastrin >200 pg/mL; secretin stimulation test (paradoxical rise).
Q11

(PGI June 2000) In achalasia cardia, true is:

Mallory-Weiss tear: longitudinal mucosal tear at GEJ from retching. Painless haematemesis.
Q12

(AIIMS May 1996) A young patient with dysphagia more for liquids than solids regurgitates food often at night. Radiography shows a rat-tailed appearance. Likely diagnosis is:

Boerhaave's syndrome: full-thickness oesophageal perforation from forceful vomiting. Mediastinitis.
Q13

(AIIMS June 2003) A young patient presents with history of dysphagia more to liquids than solids. The first investigation you will do is:

Upper GI bleed: peptic ulcer most common cause (30-40%).
Q14

(AIIMS Nov 1993) Heller's operation is done for:

Esophageal varices: IV octreotide + endoscopic banding/sclerotherapy.
Q15

In which of the following conditions is Heller's operation done?

IBD: Crohn's = transmural, skip lesions, anywhere. UC = mucosal, continuous, starts at rectum.
Q16

Diffuse esophageal spasm is best diagnosed by:

UC: rectal bleeding, bloody diarrhoea, always involves rectum.
Q17

Corkscrew esophagus is seen in which of the following conditions?

Crohn's: abdominal pain, perianal disease, fistulas, skip lesions, string sign.
Q18

A young patient has dysphagia and symptoms of GERD. The barium swallow showed a corkscrew esophagus. What would be the MOST likely diagnosis?

Extraintestinal IBD: pyoderma gangrenosum, erythema nodosum, uveitis, PSC (more in UC).
Q19

On barium swallow, 'corkscrew esophagus' is seen in which of the following?

PSC: beaded bile ducts on MRCP. Associated with UC. Increased risk of cholangiocarcinoma.
Q20

All of the following statements about Zenker's diverticulum are true EXCEPT:

Toxic megacolon: colon dilatation >6 cm + systemic toxicity. Emergency colectomy.
Q21

(AI 2001) A male aged 60 years has foul breath. He regurgitates food that was eaten 3 days ago. A gurgling sound is often heard on swallowing. Likely diagnosis is:

IBS: functional GI disorder, Rome IV criteria. No structural/biochemical abnormality.
Q22

(AI 1991) A man aged 60 years presents with intermittent dysphagia and foul breath. He regurgitates food eaten a few days ago. Likely diagnosis is:

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Celiac disease: villous atrophy, anti-tTG antibody (IgA), anti-endomysial Ab, HLA-DQ2/DQ8.
Q23

Commonest complication of Zenker's diverticulum is:

Celiac disease: malabsorption. Dermatitis herpetiformis is pathognomonic cutaneous feature.
Q24

Which of the following is TRUE about Zenker's diverticulum?

Whipple's disease: Tropheryma whipplei. PAS-positive macrophages. Treat: TMP-SMX × 1 year.
Q25

(PGI Dec) Schatzki's ring is:

SIBO: hydrogen breath test diagnostic. Treat with rifaximin.
Q26

All are true regarding Plummer-Vinson syndrome, EXCEPT:

Colon cancer: most common GI cancer in Western world. Risk: adenomatous polyps, IBD.
Q27

(AIIMS Nov) Barrett's esophagus is diagnosed by:

FAP: APC gene (chromosome 5q21). Hundreds of polyps. Prophylactic colectomy.
Q28

All except one are true regarding Barrett's oesophagus:

HNPCC (Lynch): mismatch repair genes, microsatellite instability. Right-sided colon cancer.
Q29

(AIIMS June) Barrett's esophagus can lead to:

Appendicitis: McBurney's point tenderness, Rovsing's sign, psoas sign.
Q30

(PG Dec 1998) All of the following are true about Barrett's esophagus, EXCEPT:

Diverticulosis: sigmoid colon. Diverticulitis: LLQ pain, fever, leukocytosis.
Q31

(AI) False about Barrett's esophagus is:

Ischaemic colitis: 'thumbprinting' (submucosal oedema) on CT. Watershed area = splenic flexure.
Q32

(AIIMS Nov 1994) What is true regarding Barrett's esophagus?

C. difficile colitis: after antibiotic use. Toxin A+B. Treat: metronidazole (mild) or oral vancomycin (severe).
Q33

Barrett's esophagus is commonly associated with:

Hirschsprung's: congenital aganglionosis of rectum. Absent Meissner's and Auerbach's plexuses.
Q34

(PGI 2009) Which of the following statements about Mallory-Weiss syndrome are true?

Intussusception: most common bowel obstruction in infants (6 months-2 years). Currant-jelly stool.
Q35

The most prevalent esophageal cancer worldwide is:

Volvulus: sigmoid most common (elderly). Coffee bean sign on X-ray.
Q36

(AIIMS Feb 1997) Most common site of carcinoma esophagus is:

Acute pancreatitis causes: gallstones and alcohol most common.
Q37

Most common site for squamous cell carcinoma esophagus is:

Ranson's criteria: age >55, WBC >16,000, glucose >200, LDH >350, AST >250.
Q38

(AIIMS Nov 1996, June 2000) The most common site of esophageal adenocarcinoma is:

Contrast CT: gold standard for severity assessment of pancreatitis. Balthazar score.
Q39

The commonest site of carcinoma esophagus in India is:

Pancreatic pseudocyst: fluid collection without epithelial lining. Lesser sac. Most resolve spontaneously.
Q40

(AI 1996) The following are predisposing factors for esophageal carcinoma, EXCEPT:

Chronic pancreatitis: most common cause: chronic alcoholism. Calcifications on plain X-ray.
Q41

(AIIMS Dec 1997) Hyperkeratosis of palm and sole is seen in:

Pancreatic cancer: head of pancreas (70%). Painless jaundice. Whipple procedure.
Q42

(PGI June 1999) Esophageal adenocarcinoma is predisposed by (select two best options):

Courvoisier's law: palpable gallbladder + painless jaundice ≠ gallstones → carcinoma.
Q43

Adenocarcinoma of the esophagus develops in:

Insulinoma: Whipple's triad. Most common functioning islet cell tumour. 90% benign.
Q44

(AI 1998) Adenocarcinoma of esophagus is commonly found in:

Glucagonoma: necrolytic migratory erythema, diabetes, DVT, depression. High glucagon.
Q45

(PGI June 1998, Dec 2003) All of the following statements about esophageal carcinoma are true, EXCEPT:

VIPoma (WDHA): watery diarrhoea + hypokalaemia + achlorhydria.
Q46

Which neoadjuvant chemotherapy is used in esophageal carcinoma?

Carcinoid: appendix most common site. Ileum for malignant. Carcinoid syndrome with hepatic mets.
Q47

(AIIMS May 2001) The commonest side effect of cisplatin in a patient using it for esophageal carcinoma is:

Carcinoid diagnosis: 24-hour urine 5-HIAA. Treat with octreotide.
Q48

Best substitute of esophagus after esophagectomy is:

Meckel's diverticulum: rule of 2's. Most common congenital GI anomaly.
Q49

Peptic ulcer is associated with all EXCEPT:

Meckel's scan: Tc-99m pertechnetate detects ectopic gastric mucosa.
Q50

(AIIMS Nov 1995) Which one is NOT associated with peptic ulcer?

Cholangitis (Charcot's triad): fever + jaundice + RUQ pain.
Q51

All of the following are associated with gastric ulcer, EXCEPT:

PBC: anti-mitochondrial antibody (AMA-M2) in 95%.
Q52

(AI 1992)

Budd-Chiari: hepatic vein thrombosis. Caudate lobe hypertrophy (drains directly to IVC).
Q53

(PGI Dec 2000) Stress ulcers seen in burns are:

SBP: ascitic fluid PMN ≥250/μL. E. coli most common. Treat: cefotaxime + albumin.
Q54

Helicobacter pylori is NOT associated with:

Duodenal atresia: 'double bubble' sign on X-ray. Associated with Down syndrome.
Q55

(AIIMS Nov 2004) Endoscopic biopsy from a case of H. pylori related duodenal ulcer is most likely to reveal:

Coeliac axis/superior mesenteric artery anatomy: coeliac axis supplies foregut (stomach to mid-duodenum). SMA supplies midgut. IMA supplies hindgut.
Q56

(AI 1999) H. pylori is known to cause all of the following EXCEPT:

Barrett's oesophagus: intestinal metaplasia in lower oesophagus due to chronic GERD.
Q57

Diagnostic tests for H. pylori include all of the following EXCEPT:

Oesophageal manometry: gold standard for diagnosing oesophageal motility disorders (achalasia, diffuse spasm).
Q58

Epidemiological studies of H. pylori are done by using:

Plummer-Vinson syndrome: iron deficiency anaemia + dysphagia (oesophageal web) + koilonychia. Associated with oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma.
Q59

(AIIMS June 2000) Which of the following is FALSE regarding H. pylori infection?

Zenker's diverticulum: pharyngeal diverticulum at Killian's dehiscence (weakness between thyropharyngeus and cricopharyngeus). Halitosis, regurgitation.
Q60

A patient with H. pylori infection is treated with drugs. The best method to detect presence of H. pylori in this person is:

Peutz-Jeghers syndrome: AD, STK11 mutation. Mucocutaneous melanin pigmentation + GI hamartomatous polyps + cancer risk.
Q61

The preferred test for confirming H. pylori eradication is:

Juvenile polyposis syndrome: multiple juvenile polyps, elevated cancer risk. SMAD4 or BMPR1A mutation.

🟤

Hepatology

240 Questions

▼
0/240 answered 0 correct
Q1

Jaundice per se causes all except/gastrointestinal symptoms:

HAV: RNA, fecal-oral, acute hepatitis only, no chronic disease.
Q2

Hyperbilirubinemia is seen in:

HBsAg = carrier/active infection. HBcAb = prior exposure. HBeAg = high replication. HBsAb = immunity.
Q3

Dubin Johnson occurs because it is:

HBV window period: HBsAg gone but HBsAb not yet. Only HBcAb (IgM) positive.
Q4

Jaundice is seen with elevated bilirubin levels. Indication of some shows presence of bilirubin in urine:

Chronic HBV: HBsAg positive >6 months. HBeAg = high replication.
Q5

Most patients with elevated bilirubin levels. Urine bilirubin and urine Urobilinogen is present:

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

HBV: most common cause of HCC worldwide.
Q6

Gilbert's syndrome is:

HCV: 70-80% become chronic, 20% cirrhosis. No vaccine available.
Q7

A patient presents with icterus, but there is no evidence of bilirubin in urine. What is the most likely cause of jaundice in this patient?

HCV treatment: DAAs — sofosbuvir-based. >95% SVR (cure).
Q8

A patient presents with unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia. Presence of urobilinogen in urine. Which amongst the following is the least likely cause:

HBV/HDV coinfection vs superinfection: superinfection = accelerated cirrhosis.
Q9

A patient presents with unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia and elevated urobilinogen levels. The most likely diagnosis is:

HEV: fecal-oral, most common acute viral hepatitis in developing countries, high mortality in pregnancy.
Q10

A 50-year-old man presents with jaundice, pruritus, and a palpable gallbladder. The most likely diagnosis is:

HEV pregnancy mortality: particularly 3rd trimester. Fulminant hepatic failure.
Q11

A 35-year-old man presents with jaundice. Blood examination shows total bilirubin 10 mg/dl, direct bilirubin 2 mg/dl, alkaline phosphatase 180 KA units. Diagnosis is:

Alcoholic liver disease: fatty liver (reversible) → alcoholic hepatitis → cirrhosis.
Q12

A 25-year-old man presents with jaundice. Total bilirubin is 10 mg/dl, alkaline Ph 8.4 units. Diagnosis is:

Alcoholic hepatitis: AST:ALT ratio >2:1. Treat: prednisolone if MDF >32.
Q13

True statement about unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia:

Maddrey's DF: 4.6 × (PT-control) + bilirubin (mg/dL). >32 = severe → steroids or pentoxifylline.
Q14

In obstructive jaundice, prothrombin time is prolonged. It:

NAFLD: most common chronic liver disease in Western world. Associated with metabolic syndrome.
Q15

Abnormal prothrombin time of hepatocytes may be assessed by:

NASH: inflammation ± fibrosis, can progress to cirrhosis. Liver biopsy for staging.
Q16

Increased Br. level is seen in all, except:

Wilson's disease: AR, ATP7B (chromosome 13). KF rings, haemolytic anaemia, neuropsychiatric.
Q17

A 50-year-old man presents to the internet with fever. The patient is receiving intravenous antibiotics and has only symptomatic complaints. On examination, jaundice is seen. Hepatitis serology is: - HBsAg: negative, Anti-HBc: positive, Anti-HBs: positive The patient has no significant past medical history. The most likely diagnosis is:

Wilson's labs: low ceruloplasmin, high urine copper (>100 μg/24 hr), KF rings on slit lamp.
Q18

All of the following are true about Dubin Johnson syndrome:

Haemochromatosis: HFE gene (C282Y), chromosome 6p, AR. Iron overload.
Q19

The most common cause of Dubin Johnson syndrome is:

Haemochromatosis: bronze diabetes, arthropathy (2nd-3rd MCP), cardiac arrhythmias, hypogonadism.
Q20

A 25-year-old male with a history of jaundice. Which of the following drugs is contraindicated:

Haemochromatosis labs: elevated ferritin, transferrin saturation >45% (best screening test).
Q21

Rotor syndrome is characterized by:

Haemochromatosis treatment: phlebotomy is first-line. Deferoxamine only if phlebotomy not possible.
Q22

Dubin Johnson syndrome is characterized by:

Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency: PiZZ phenotype, emphysema + cirrhosis. PAS-positive, diastase-resistant globules.
Q23

Most common genotype of hepatitis B in India is:

Autoimmune hepatitis: anti-SMA (Type 1), anti-LKM-1 (Type 2). Responds to steroids + azathioprine.
Q24

Double stranded Enveloped DNA virus:

PBC: AMA-M2 antibody (95%), cholestatic, middle-aged women. Pruritus, xanthelasma.
Q25

Hepatitis B virus is:

PSC: beaded biliary tree on MRCP, associated with UC, increased cholangiocarcinoma risk.
Q26

A 35-year-old male presented with history of jaundice, malaise, anorexia, and increased appetite. Liver function tests show: SGOT 1500, SGPT 1800, total bilirubin 10 mg/dl, direct bilirubin 2 mg/dl, serum alkaline phosphatase 200 IU/L. What is the most likely diagnosis:

Cirrhosis complications: oesophageal varices, ascites, SBP, HRS, encephalopathy, HCC.
Q27

Complications of hepatitis includes all except:

Portal HTN: portal pressure >12 mmHg.
Q28

A 25-year-old hepatitis C virus presenting to:

Hepatic encephalopathy: ammonia hypothesis. Treat: lactulose + rifaximin. Precipitants: GI bleed, infection, sedatives.
Q29

Chronic hepatitis is caused by (select two options):

Ascites SAAG ≥1.1 = portal HTN-related. SAAG <1.1 = non-portal HTN (malignancy, TB).
Q30

Which of the following hepatitis viruses have significant perinatal transmission:

SBP: cefotaxime + albumin. Prophylaxis: norfloxacin if previous SBP or Child C.
Q31

Hepatitis E is:

HRS: terlipressin + albumin is treatment.
Q32

Prophylaxis is available for all of the following, except:

Child-Pugh: encephalopathy, ascites, bilirubin, albumin, PT. Grade A/B/C.
Q33

Most common route of transmission of hepatitis B is:

MELD score: predicts 90-day mortality. Uses creatinine, bilirubin, INR.
Q34

Which of the following is the most sensitive marker of hepatitis B infection:

Liver transplant: MELD ≥15, acute liver failure, HCC within Milan criteria.
Q35

The earliest marker of hepatitis B infection is:

HCC: most common cause worldwide = HBV. AFP elevated in 70%.
Q36

A patient presents with jaundice. HBsAg positive, HBeAg positive. This indicates:

HCC screening: 6-monthly USS ± AFP in cirrhotic patients.
Q37

In a patient with Anti HBsAg is positive in serum but anti-HBc markers are negative. This indicates:

Budd-Chiari: hepatic vein thrombosis → caudate lobe hypertrophy.
Q38

A 25-year-old male is found to be positive for HBsAg, HBeAg, and anti-HBc. The biochemical profile shows elevated ALT and AST. The most likely diagnosis is:

Veno-occlusive disease: post-BMT complication.
Q39

After one year post-exposure to hepatitis B virus, a patient is found to have anti-HBs positive, anti- HBc positive, and HBsAg negative. This indicates:

Acute liver failure: coagulopathy (INR >1.5) + encephalopathy within 26 weeks.
Q40

Chronic hepatitis B infection with low infectivity is characterized by:

Most common cause of ALF in developed countries: acetaminophen overdose. Treat: NAC.
Q41

A 30-year-old male with HBsAg positive for more than 6 months. ALT is elevated. HBV DNA is 100,000 copies/ml. The most likely diagnosis is:

King's College Criteria for poor prognosis in ALF (acetaminophen): pH <7.30 OR PT >100s + Cr >3.4 + grade 3-4 encephalopathy.
Q42

Active infection of hepatitis B virus is diagnosed by:

Jaundice: pre-hepatic (indirect ↑ = haemolysis, Gilbert's). Post-hepatic (direct ↑ = obstruction).
Q43

Acute Hepatitis B infection can be best differentiated from chronic Hepatitis B by:

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Gilbert's syndrome: mild unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia, fasting-induced. UGT1A1 polymorphism. Benign.
Q44

Hepatitis B can be transmitted through all of the following except:

Crigler-Najjar Type I: absent UGT1A1, kernicterus. Type II: partial, responds to phenobarbital.
Q45

The risk of vertical transmission of hepatitis B may be as high as:

Dubin-Johnson: conjugated hyperbilirubinaemia, dark granular pigment in hepatocytes. Benign.
Q46

The best virological marker following acute infection is:

Rotor syndrome: similar to Dubin-Johnson but no pigment and normal oral cholecystogram.
Q47

The presence of anti-HBeAg in hepatitis B infection is indicative of:

ALT > AST: viral hepatitis. AST:ALT >2: alcoholic liver disease. ALP + GGT: cholestasis.
Q48

The presence of HBeAg in serum indicates:

GGT: elevated in alcohol use. Best indicator of alcohol consumption.
Q49

Which of the following is a marker for hepatitis B virus replication:

ALP elevated in: liver/biliary disease, bone disease (Paget's, metastases), pregnancy.
Q50

The following are true except:

Cholestasis: elevated conjugated bilirubin, ALP/GGT, bile in urine, pruritus.
Q51

A patient is found to be positive for HBsAg. All other hepatitis B markers are negative. The patient is asymptomatic. What is the most likely diagnosis:

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Liver biopsy: staging fibrosis in chronic liver disease, hepatic granulomas.
Q52

The following are markers of acute hepatitis B infection:

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

HBV vaccination: recombinant HBsAg vaccine. 3 doses: 0, 1, 6 months. Anti-HBs ≥10 IU/L = protective.
Q53

HBsAg is a marker of:

Hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg): appears first, 1-6 weeks post-exposure. Persists in chronic infection.
Q54

HBsAg is positive in:

Anti-HBc IgM: indicates acute HBV infection (including window period when HBsAg may be negative).
Q55

Most common cause of acute liver failure is:

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

HBe antigen: marker of active viral replication and high infectivity in HBV.
Q56

Anti-HBc IgM is seen in:

Liver in heart failure (cardiac cirrhosis/cardiac hepatopathy): nutmeg liver (centrilobular congestion), then fibrosis.
Q58

A patient is found to be positive only for Anti-HBc. This indicates:

Ascites: protein-rich exudate (>2.5 g/dL) in Budd-Chiari, portal vein thrombosis. Transudate (<2.5 g/dL) in cirrhosis.
Q59

The marker for determining efficacy of hepatitis B vaccination is:

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Hepatic encephalopathy precipitation: GI bleed (blood as nitrogen load), infection, constipation, hypokalaemia, sedatives, renal failure.
Q60

Which of the following serum markers is indicative of HBV vaccination:

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Lactulose mechanism: converts NH3 to NH4+ (non-absorbable), accelerates gut transit.
Q61

The only serological marker present in the "window period" of hepatitis B infection is:

Fatty liver in alcoholic: due to impaired fatty acid oxidation (NADH excess shifts metabolism). Microvesicular in acute fatty liver.
Q62

All of the following should be included during prophylaxis of a case of occupational exposure to HBV

Hepatic steatosis: 5% or more hepatocytes showing fatty change. Ultrasound can detect if >30%.
Q63

A 30-year-old patient presented with history of fever, jaundice, and malaise. HBsAg positive, HBeAg positive, HBV DNA 100,000 copies/ml. The most likely diagnosis is:

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

This question requires options to be added. Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine for the answer.

Hepatitis B infectivity is indicated by:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q65

Anti-HBcAg is indicated by all of the following, except:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q66

Hepatitis B virus is:

Refer to Mudit Khanna Medicine textbook for the detailed answer and explanation.
Q67

The following are true about hepatitis B virus except:

Hematology

500 Questions

▼
0/500 answered 0 correct
Q1

Which of the following conditions is associated with microcytic hypochromic anemia?

Iron deficiency anaemia: most common anaemia worldwide. Microcytic hypochromic. Low ferritin, low serum iron, high TIBC.
Q2

All of the following cause microcytic hypochromic anaemia, except:

Serum ferritin: best single test to diagnose IDA. Acute phase reactant (can be falsely elevated in inflammation).
Q3

Which of the following cause Microcytic Hypochromic anemia?

IDA treatment: oral ferrous sulfate 325 mg TID.
Q4

Which of the following are not associated with microcytic hypochromic RRCs on Peripheral smear?

Pernicious anaemia: autoimmune destruction of parietal cells → absent intrinsic factor → B12 malabsorption.
Q5

Elevated serum ferritin, decreased serum iron and % transferring saturation are most consistent with the diagnosis of:

B12 deficiency: megaloblastic anaemia + SCD (posterior + lateral spinal cord columns) + glossitis. Hypersegmented neutrophils.
Q6

Which of the following is classified as a Hyperproliferative anemia with a High Reticulocyte Count?

Schilling test: Stage I (oral radioB12 alone). Stage II (+ intrinsic factor). Abnormal Stage I + normal Stage II = pernicious anaemia.
Q7

RDW is an indication of which of the following:

Folate deficiency: megaloblastic anaemia WITHOUT neurological symptoms.
Q8

Which of the following Microcytic Anemias is associated with a High Red Cell Distribution Width?

SCD: HbS (β6 Glu→Val), AR. Haemolytic anaemia + vaso-occlusive crises.
Q9

Which of the following is not expected in a case of Microcytic Hypochromic Anemia:

SCD complications: vaso-occlusive crises, acute chest syndrome, stroke, aplastic crisis (Parvovirus B19).
Q10

Which of the following Microcytic Anemias is associated with a Normal Free Eryhtocyte Protoporphyrin Levels?

Acute chest syndrome in SCD: new pulmonary infiltrate. Treat: exchange transfusion, bronchodilators, O2.
Q11

Which of the following is not expected in a case of Microcytic Hypochromic Anemia

Hydroxyurea: increases HbF → reduces sickling. Decreases frequency of vaso-occlusive crises.
Q12

A patient presents with Microcytic Hypochromic anemia. Serum iron levels and TIBC are decreased. Likely diagnosis is:

Thalassaemia: reduced/absent globin chain synthesis. Microcytic hypochromic, target cells, elevated HbA2.
Q13

Anemia of Chronic disease can be differentiated from Iron deficiency anemia by:

Beta-thalassaemia major: transfusion-dependent, frontal bossing, 'hair-on-end' skull X-ray, iron overload.
Q14

A child presents with hypochromic microcytic anemia, with normal levels of free Erythrocyte Protoporphyrin The most likely diagnosis is:

HbA2 elevated (>3.5%): beta thalassaemia trait.
Q15

Elevated serum ferritin, serum iron and percent transferrin saturation are most consistent with the diagnosis of :

Alpha thalassaemia: 4-gene deletion = Hb Bart's hydrops fetalis (incompatible with life).
Q16

Microcytic Hypochromic Anemia with an elevated Red Cell Count should suggest a diagnosis of:

HbH disease: 3-gene deletion, moderate haemolytic anaemia, splenomegaly.
Q17

Mentzer index more than 13 suggests a diagnosis of :

Hereditary spherocytosis: AD, spectrin defect. Microspherocytes, ↑MCHC, negative Coombs.
Q18

A child has Hb-6.5 gm%, MCV-65, MCH-15 and protoporphyrin with red cell distribution width much less is most likely to be suffering from :

G6PD deficiency: X-linked. Triggered by oxidant stress (primaquine, dapsone, fava beans). Bite cells, Heinz bodies.
Q19

A student from Punjab presents for evaluation of anemia. His RDW was observed to be 13.5 while the MCV was 62.5 fl. The likely diagnosis is

AIHA: DAT/direct Coombs positive. Warm (IgG) vs cold (IgM, cold agglutinin disease).
Q20

A 16 year old young girl presents with a history of fatiguability weakness and lethargy. Complete Blood picture (CBC) reveals Haemoglobin of 7.0, MCV of 70, MCH of 20 pg/cell and Red cell Distribution Width (RDW) of 20. The most likely diagnosis is:

Warm AIHA (IgG): SLE, CLL, lymphoma, methyldopa. Treat: steroids, splenectomy.
Q21

A nine month old boy of Sindhi parents presented to you with complaints of progressive lethargy, irritability & pallor since 6 months of age. Examination revealed severe pallor. Investigation showed Hb-3.8 mg%; MCV-58 fl; MCH-19.4 pg/cell. Blood film shows anisocytosis, occasional target cells and increase in normoblasts. Osmotic fragility is normal. X-ray skull shows expansion of erythroid marrow. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

Cold agglutinin disease (IgM): Mycoplasma pneumoniae, EBV. Keep patient warm.
Q22

A 30 year old female being evaluated for anemia reveals the following indices: RBC count = 4.5 x 1012/L; MCV 55fl; TLC 8000. There is no history of blood transfusion. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis:

PNH: PIG-A gene mutation → GPI anchor deficiency → complement-mediated lysis. Dark morning urine, thrombosis.
Q23

All of the following are causes of iron deficiency anemia, Except:

PNH diagnosis: flow cytometry showing absent CD55/CD59. Ham's test (older method).
Q24

Erythropoetin therapy in patients with Chronic Renal Failure is associated with increased risk of

Aplastic anaemia: pancytopenia + hypocellular bone marrow. Idiopathic most common.
Q25

A patient with anemia has the following indices. MCV = 60; Hb = 5gm%; MCHC = 20. The most likely cause of his anemia is:

Aplastic anaemia treatment: immunosuppression (ATG + cyclosporine) or allogeneic HSCT.
Q26

Iron absorption is increased in all except:

Diamond-Blackfan: pure red cell aplasia. Triphalangeal thumbs. Elevated RBC adenosine deaminase.
Q27

Signs and symptoms considered highly specific for iron deficiency INCLUDE ALL OF THE FOLLOWING, EXCEPT:

Fanconi's anaemia: AR, DNA repair defect. Absent thumbs/radii, skin pigmentation, cancer risk.
Q28

Triad of Plummer Vinson Syndrome includes all of the following, except:

Polycythaemia vera: JAK2 V617F mutation (97%). ↑RBC mass, WBC, platelets, splenomegaly, pruritus after bath.
Q29

Which of the following is true for PlummerVinson syndrome true is-

PV complications: thrombosis (most common cause of death), haemorrhage, transformation to MF or AML.
Q30

Which of the following findings is diagnostic of iron deficiency anemia :

Essential thrombocythaemia: platelets >450×10⁹/L. JAK2/CALR/MPL mutations.
Q31

All of the following are features of iron deficiency anemia, except:

Primary myelofibrosis: tear-drop cells, leukoerythroblastic blood film, 'dry tap' on BM aspiration.
Q32

Which of the following is true about Iron Deficiency Anemia

CML: Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22) = BCR-ABL. WBC >50,000 with left shift, basophilia.
Q33

Most sensitive and specific test for diagnosis of iron deficiency is:

CML treatment: imatinib (first-generation TKI). Allogeneic HSCT for blast crisis.
Q34

The earliest sign of iron deficiency anaemia:

AML: most common acute leukaemia in adults. Auer rods (especially M3/APL).
Q35

Which of the following statements about iron deficiency anemia is true:

APL (AML-M3): t(15;17), PML-RARA, DIC. Treat: ATRA + arsenic trioxide.
Q36

Best marker for iron deficiency is:

ALL: most common cancer in children (2-5 years). Treat: multi-drug chemotherapy + CNS prophylaxis.
Q37

The earliest recognizable change of erythrocytes in iron-deficiency anemia is

ALL treatment: induction (steroids + vincristine + asparaginase) + consolidation + maintenance.
Q38

The most suitable test to assess iron stores is:

CLL: most common adult leukaemia in Western world. CD5+ B cells, smudge cells. Rai/Binet staging.
Q39

How many milligrams of iron is usually present asiron stores in adult females?

CLL treatment: ibrutinib, FCR. Richter's transformation to DLBCL is a complication.
Q40

Which is not seen in Iron deficiency anaemia:

Hairy cell leukaemia: TRAP positive. Pancytopenia. Treat: cladribine.
Q41

Which of the following best represents the sequence of events in Iron Deficiency anemia

Multiple myeloma: CRAB = Hypercalcaemia, Renal failure, Anaemia, Bone lesions (lytic).
Q42

All of the following statements about oral iron therapy are true, except:

Myeloma diagnosis: BM plasma cells >10% + M protein + end-organ damage (CRAB).
Q43

Which of the following is true about oral therapy for iron deficiency anemia:

Myeloma treatment: bortezomib + lenalidomide + dexamethasone (VRd). Autologous HSCT.
Q44

All of the following are indications of Intravenous Iron Administration, Except

Waldenström's: IgM paraprotein + lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma. Hyperviscosity syndrome.
Q45

Iron Dextran Preparations may be used for all of the following conditions, except

Hodgkin's vs Non-Hodgkin's lymphoma: HL has Reed-Sternberg cells.
Q46

Which of the following is associated with highest risk of Anaphylaxis

Hodgkin's lymphoma: Reed-Sternberg cells. Nodular sclerosis most common type.
Q47

Iron overload occurs in all, except :

DLBCL: most common aggressive NHL. Follicular lymphoma: most common indolent NHL.
Q48

All are true regarding Anaemia of Chronic Diseases, except :

Burkitt's lymphoma: t(8;14), MYC translocation. 'Starry sky' pattern. Most aggressive.
Q49

Which of the following is true about Anemia of Chronic Disease:

Ann Arbor staging: I (single), II (same side), III (both sides diaphragm), IV (extranodal).
Q50

Which of the following is seen in chronic inflammatory anemia

Coagulation: intrinsic (aPTT) = XII,XI,IX,VIII. Extrinsic (PT/INR) = VII, TF. Common = X,V,II,I.
Q51

Anemia of chronic disease is characterized by all, except:

Haemophilia A: factor VIII deficiency. X-linked. Prolonged aPTT, normal PT. Haemarthroses.
Q52

A 25-year-old lady on treatment for Rheumatoid Arthritis has the following laboratory findings. Hemoglobin of 9 gm/dL; MCV-50 fl; Ferritin 200μg/L; TIBC 274 μg/L; Serum iron 30μg/dL. What is the likely

Haemophilia B (Christmas disease): factor IX deficiency. X-linked. Same presentation as haemophilia A.
Q53

All of the following are true about Anemia of chronic failure Except:

VWD: most common inherited bleeding disorder. Type 1 (partial), Type 2 (qualitative), Type 3 (severe).
Q54

Anemia in chronic renal failure (CRF) is due to:

DIC: PT ↑, aPTT ↑, fibrinogen ↓, D-dimer ↑, thrombocytopenia, fragmented RBCs (schistocytes).
Q55

All of the following statements about X-Linked Sideroblastic Anemia are true, except

HIT type II: IgG against heparin-PF4 complex. Stop all heparin; switch to argatroban.
Q56

Not present in Sideroblastic anaemia is :

ITP: anti-GPIIb/IIIa antibodies. Isolated thrombocytopenia. Treat: steroids → IVIG → splenectomy.
Q57

A patient presents with increased serum iron, decreased TIBC, increased percent saturation and increased serum ferritin. Most probable diagnosis is :

TTP: ADAMTS13 deficiency. Thrombocytopenia + MAHA + neurological + fever + renal. Treat: plasma exchange.
Q58

Which of the following is the most likelycause of Non-Megaloblastic Macrocytic Anemia

HUS vs TTP: HUS = children, E. coli O157:H7, renal predominant. TTP = adults, neurological, ADAMTS13 deficiency.
Q59

Macrocytic anemia is least likely in all of the following conditions except :

Most dangerous transfusion reaction: acute haemolytic reaction (ABO incompatibility).
Q60

Macrocytic anemia may be caused by all of the following except :

Group O: universal donor (no antigens, has anti-A and anti-B). Group AB: universal recipient.
Q61

Megaloblastic anemia due to folic acid deficiency is commonly due to:

Rh system: Rh-negative mother + Rh-positive fetus → HDN. Prevent with anti-D immunoglobulin (RhoGAM).
Q62

Deficiency of the 'intrinsic factor of Castle' causes:

Very high ESR (>100 mm/hr): TB, myeloma, giant cell arteritis, Waldenström's.
Q63

Primary mechanism of Megaloblastic anemia in blind loop syndrome is due to:

CRP: rises within 6-12 hours, more sensitive and specific than ESR for acute inflammation.
Q64

Which of the following types of anemia is associated with a Raised MCV and Normal MCHC:

G6PD: fluorescent spot test and G6PD assay for diagnosis.
Q65

A patient has Hb 6gm/%, folic acid 8 ng/ml, vitamin B12 60pg/ml, serum iron 160μg/dl, and MCV-104. The diagnosis is:

Anaemia of chronic disease: normocytic, low serum iron, LOW/NORMAL TIBC, HIGH ferritin. Due to hepcidin.
Q66

A Patient presents with macroglossia and loss of tongue papilla. His Hb is 11.5 and MCV is 100. What should be the next step in investigating this patient?

Sideroblastic anaemia: ring sideroblasts in BM. Causes: MDS, lead poisoning, alcohol, pyridoxine deficiency.
Q67

Schilling's test is used to determine deficiency of

Lead poisoning: basophilic stippling of RBCs. Chelation with DMSA (succimer).
Q68

Schilling's test may be used to establish a diagnosis of :

MDS: clonal disorder, cytopenias, dysplastic cells. Risk of AML transformation. Treat: azacitidine/decitabine.
Q69

Megaloblastic anemia should be treated with both folic acid vitamin B12 because :

Haemolytic anaemia: reticulocytosis, indirect hyperbilirubinaemia, ↑LDH, reduced haptoglobin.
Q70

All of the following cause intravascular hemolysis, Except:

Intravascular haemolysis: haemoglobinuria, haemosiderinuria, methaemalbuminaemia. PNH, transfusion reactions.
Q71

Hemoglobinuria may be seen in all of the following conditions Except:

Extravascular haemolysis: cells destroyed in RES. Splenomegaly, indirect jaundice.
