Endocrinology

353 Questions

▼
0/353 answered 0 correct
Q1

Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia (MEN) Type-I is also known as

T1DM: autoimmune beta cell destruction, absolute insulin deficiency. HLA-DR3/DR4. Childhood onset, DKA.
Q2

Sipple syndrome is also known as:

T2DM: insulin resistance + relative insulin deficiency. Obesity, metabolic syndrome.
Q3

A 20 year old male presented with chronic constipation, headache and palpitations. On examination he had marfanoid habitus, neuromas of tongue, medullated corneal nerve fibers and nodule of 2x2 cm size in the left lobe of thyroid gland. This patient is a case of:

HbA1c: reflects 2-3 months. Normal <5.7%; Prediabetes 5.7-6.4%; Diabetes ≥6.5%.
Q4

In MEN I, which is seen most commonly:

DKA: glucose ↑, ketones ↑, metabolic acidosis. pH <7.3, HCO3 <15. Kussmaul breathing, fruity breath.
Q5

Which of the following endocrine tumors is most commonly seen in MEN-1:

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

DKA treatment: IV normal saline + IV regular insulin + potassium replacement.
Q6

MEN I syndrome is associated with all of the following, Except

HHS: glucose >600, osmolality >320, minimal ketosis, altered consciousness. No Kussmaul breathing.
Q7

Type I MEN involves all, except:

Dawn phenomenon: early morning hyperglycaemia from nocturnal GH surge.
Q8

MEN I syndrome is most commonly associated with:

Diabetic neuropathy: most common diabetic complication. Distal symmetric polyneuropathy most common.
Q9

Type I MEN involves all, EXCEPT:

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Diabetic nephropathy: microalbuminuria earliest sign. ACE-I/ARBs slow progression.
Q10

A 36 year old female with symptoms of hyperparathyroidism, pituitary adenomas, islet cell tumor with cutaneous angiofibromas. What is the diagnosis?

Diabetic retinopathy: most common cause of blindness in working-age adults in developed countries.
Q11

All are associated with MEN 2 except:

Hypoglycaemia unawareness: loss of warning symptoms from repeated hypoglycaemia.
Q12

All are associated with MEN-2, Except:

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Metformin: first-line T2DM. Reduces hepatic glucose output. Lactic acidosis risk in renal failure.
Q13

All of the following are features of MEN II a, except:

SGLT2 inhibitors: cardiovascular and renal protective. Side effects: UTI, genital infections, DKA.
Q14

Which of the following implies the combination of Medullary thyroid carcinoma, Pheochromocytoma, and Hyper-parathyroidism?

GLP-1 agonists: enhance insulin secretion, suppress glucagon. Nausea/vomiting side effects.
Q15

True about Sipple syndrome is?

DPP-4 inhibitors: weight neutral, low hypoglycaemia risk.
Q16

All of the following are seen in MEN II B, Except:

Hypothyroidism: TSH elevated, free T4 low. Fatigue, weight gain, bradycardia, delayed reflexes.
Q17

CDKN1B gene mutation is implicated in:

Most common hypothyroidism in developed countries: Hashimoto's thyroiditis.
Q18

A patient presents with intermittent headache. On examination there is hypertension and a thyroid nodule. Which of the following steps is to be taken next:

Myxoedema coma: IV T4 + T3 + hydrocortisone.
Q19

A 25 year old young woman has recurrent episodes of headache and sweating. Her mother had renal calculi and died after having a neck mass. The physical examination reveals a thyroid nodule but no clinical sign of thyrotoxicosis. Before performing thyroid surgery, the surgeon should order:

Hyperthyroidism: TSH suppressed, free T4/T3 elevated.
Q20

Schmidt syndrome includes all of the following, except:

Graves' disease: TSH receptor stimulating antibodies (TRAb/TSI). Diffuse goitre + exophthalmos + pretibial myxoedema.
Q21

Which of the following Autoimmune Polyglandular Syndrome (APS) presents with Candidiasis, Adrenal Insufficiency and Hypoparathyroidism:

Thyroid storm: fever, tachycardia. Treat: PTU + Lugol's iodine + propranolol + dexamethasone.
Q22

The most common cause of Cushing's syndrome is:

Thyroid cancer: papillary most common (80%). Medullary = RET gene, calcitonin secreting.
Q23

A common cause of Cushing syndrome is:

Medullary thyroid carcinoma: calcitonin secreting C-cells. MEN2A and MEN2B. RET gene.
Q24

Intake of exogenous steroid causes:

Cushing's syndrome: cortisol excess. Truncal obesity, striae, proximal myopathy, HTN, osteoporosis.
Q25

Cushing syndrome is not a feature of:

Cushing's disease: pituitary ACTH-secreting adenoma. Most common cause of Cushing's syndrome.
Q26

All of the following are clinical features of Cushing's syndrome except:

Cushing's diagnosis: 24-hour urine free cortisol, late-night salivary cortisol, low-dose dexamethasone suppression test.
Q27

The most likely diagnosis for a lady with central obesity showing purple striae on abdominal skin is:

High-dose dexamethasone suppression: suppression >50% → pituitary Cushing's.
Q28

All of the following are true about Cushing's syndrome, Except

Nelson's syndrome: pituitary tumour growth after bilateral adrenalectomy. Hyperpigmentation + high ACTH.
Q29

Cushing syndrome is characterized by all except:

Addison's disease: autoimmune (most common, anti-21-hydroxylase antibodies). Low cortisol, high ACTH → hyperpigmentation.
Q30

Features not seen in Cushing's Syndrome is:

Adrenal crisis: hypotension, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, hypoglycaemia. IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat.
Q31

All are features of Cushing's disease except:

Waterhouse-Friderichsen: bilateral adrenal haemorrhage in meningococcal sepsis.
Q32

Cushing's disease is associated with:

CAH: 21-hydroxylase deficiency (95%). Salt-wasting: ↑17-OHP, ↑androgens, ↓cortisol, ↓aldosterone.
Q33

About Cushing syndrome, true is:

11-beta hydroxylase deficiency: hypertension + female virilisation.
Q34

A patient with cushinoid features presents with hemoptysis; he shows no response to dexamethasone suppression test; most likely diagnosis here is:

Phaeochromocytoma: rule of 10s. Catecholamine-secreting tumour of adrenal medulla.
Q35

Lalloo, 50 years old, a chronic smoker, presents with history of hemoptysis. He was having truncal obesity and hypertension. He had an elevated ACTH level which was not suppressible with high dose dexamethasone. What would be the most probable diagnosis:

Phaeochromocytoma diagnosis: 24-hour urine metanephrines (most sensitive). Locate with MIBG scan.
Q36

A chronic smoker presented with mild haemoptysis. He also gave a history of hypertension and obesity. The Lab data showed raised ACTH levels, which were not suppressed by high dose dexamethasone. The cause for the Cushing's syndrome in the patient is:

Hyperaldosteronism (Conn's): HTN + hypokalaemia + metabolic alkalosis. Aldosterone:renin ratio >30.
Q37

The differentiating feature b/w Ectopic ACTH secretion and Cushing syndrome is:

Primary hyperaldosteronism: most common surgically correctable cause of hypertension.
Q38

Which of the following is the earliest manifestation of Cushing's syndrome?

MEN 1: pituitary + parathyroid + pancreatic tumours (3 P's). MEN1 gene (chromosome 11q13).
Q39

A 28 year old lady has put on weight (10 kg over a period of 3 years), and has oligomenorrhoea followed by amenorrhoea for 8 months. The blood pressure is 160/100 mm of Hg. Which of the following is the most appropriate investigation?

MEN 2A: MTC + phaeochromocytoma + parathyroid hyperplasia. RET mutation.
Q40

Conn's syndrome is most commonly associated with:

MEN 2B: MTC + phaeochromocytoma + mucosal neuromas + Marfanoid habitus. RET mutation.
Q41

All of the following may be associated with primary aldosteronism, Except:

Primary hyperparathyroidism: most common cause of hypercalcaemia in outpatients. Usually parathyroid adenoma.
Q42

Conn's syndrome is associated with all, except:

Hypercalcaemia: stones + bones + groans + psychic moans.
Q43

Increased Aldosterone leads to all except:

Severe hypercalcaemia: IV saline + bisphosphonates. Calcitonin for rapid effect.
Q44

NOT a feature of primary hyperaldosteronism is

Hypoparathyroidism: low PTH → hypocalcaemia + hyperphosphataemia. Post-thyroid surgery most common.
Q45

In Conn's syndrome the following are true, except:

Pseudohypoparathyroidism: PTH resistance. Low Ca, high PO4, HIGH PTH. Type 1a = Albright's hereditary osteodystrophy.
Q46

Excess Aldosterone is associated with all the following except:

Acromegaly: GH excess in adults after epiphyseal fusion. IGF-1 elevated. Coarse features, large extremities.
Q47

Excess Aldosterone is associated with all the following except:

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Acromegaly diagnosis: IGF-1 elevated + failure of GH suppression after 75g oral glucose.
Q48

Which is not a feature of primary hyperaldosteronism:

Gigantism: GH excess in children (before epiphyseal fusion) → tall stature.
Q49

The following are feature of primary hyperaldosteronism:

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Prolactinoma: most common pituitary adenoma. Galactorrhoea + amenorrhoea. Treat: cabergoline/bromocriptine.
Q50

True about Conn's syndrome (select two best options):

DI: polyuria + polydipsia + dilute urine. Water deprivation test confirms.
Q51

True about primary aldosteronism (select three options):

SIADH causes: CNS disease, SCLC, pain, nausea, drugs (carbamazepine, SSRIs, cyclophosphamide).
Q52

A young hypertensive patient has serum K+ 2.8 meq/L and Aldosterone level with ↓ed plasma rennin activity. The likely cause is:

SIADH: euvolaemic hyponatraemia, low serum osmolality, high urine Na (>40), high urine osmolality.
Q53

In primary adrenal hyperplasia, incorrect is:

PTH: increases serum calcium by bone resorption, renal reabsorption, 1,25-OH-D synthesis.
Q54

In primary adrenal hyperplasia, incorrect is

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Vitamin D deficiency: rickets (children), osteomalacia (adults). Low Ca, phosphate, high ALP.
Q55

Most common cause of adrenal insufficiency in India is

Osteoporosis: T-score ≤-2.5 by DEXA. Bisphosphonates + calcium + vitamin D.
Q56

Most common cause of Addison's Disease in India is:

Paget's disease of bone: elevated ALP, bowing of tibia, deafness. Bisphosphonates.
Q57

Which of the following is the most common cause of Adrenal gland infection in patients with HIV:

Osteopetrosis: osteoclast failure → dense brittle bones. Pancytopenia, CN compression.
Q58

Feature of addison's disease include all of the following except:

Primary amenorrhoea: no menses by age 15. Evaluate: FSH, LH, prolactin, TSH, pregnancy test.
Q59

All of the following are true about Adrenal Crisis, except:

Turner's syndrome: 45,X. Short stature, primary amenorrhoea, webbed neck, coarctation of aorta.
Q60

Primary Adrenal Insufficiency causes all of the following, Except

Klinefelter's syndrome: 47,XXY. Tall, hypogonadism, small testes, gynaecomastia, infertility.
Q61

Chronic adrenal insufficiency is characterized by all of the following:

PCOS: oligomenorrhoea + hyperandrogenism + polycystic ovaries. LH:FSH ratio >2:1. Most common cause of female infertility.

Connective Tissue

262 Questions

0/262 answered 0 correct
Q1

All of the following are small vessel vasculitis, Except: Wegner's granulomatosis Polyarteritis Nodosa Henoch-Schonelin purpura

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

RA: symmetric polyarthritis, PIP/MCP joints, morning stiffness >1 hr. RF and anti-CCP antibodies.
Q2

Which of the following is a 'Small Vessel Vasculitis': Polyarteritis Nodosa (PAN) Microscopic polyangiitis Giant cell vasculitis Takayasu's Disease

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Anti-CCP: more specific than RF for RA. Positive in early RA, predicts erosive disease.
Q3

All of the following are small vessels vasculitis, Except: Classical PAN HSP Churg Strauss Syndrome Microscopic Polyangitis

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

RA extra-articular: nodules, pleuritis, pericarditis, scleritis, Felty's syndrome.
Q4

Which of the following are Small Vessel Vacuitides (Select two best options): Kawasaki disease Churg-Strauss Syndrome

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Felty's syndrome: RA + splenomegaly + granulocytopenia. High infection risk.
Q5

ANCA is found in all of the following Except: Churg-Strauss disease Takayasu arteritis

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

RA treatment: methotrexate (anchor DMARD). Anti-TNF biologics for refractory.
Q6

ANCA positive vasculitis include all of the following Except: Churg strauss syndrome Microscopic PAN Good pasture's syndrome

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

SLE: young women, butterfly malar rash, photosensitivity, serositis, nephritis, cytopenias.
Q7

ANCA is NOT associated with which of the following disease: Wegener's Granulomatosis Henoch Schonlein Purpura Churg Strauss syndrome

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

SLE SLICC criteria: ≥4 of 11 criteria.
Q8

ANCA is NOT associated with which of the following diseases : Wegener's granulomatosis

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

ANA: very sensitive for SLE (95-98%). Anti-dsDNA: specific for SLE (70%), correlates with disease activity.
Q9

ACNA is sensitive and specific for: Post streptococcal glomerulonephritis Idiopathic crescentic glomerulonephritis. Henoch's schonlein purpura

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Anti-Smith (anti-Sm): most specific for SLE (25% sensitivity). Anti-Ro/La: SLE, Sjögren's, neonatal lupus.
Q10

CANCA Positivity is specific for: Henoch shontein purpura

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Lupus nephritis class III/IV: treat with cyclophosphamide or MMF + steroids.
Q11

c-ANCA is characteristic for : R.P.G.N. Henoch's Schonlein Purpura Wegeners granulomatosis

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Drug-induced lupus: hydralazine, procainamide, INH. Anti-histone Ab. No renal/CNS. Resolves when drug stopped.
Q12

C-ANCA is associated with: Churg- Strauss Syndrome

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

APS: recurrent thrombosis + pregnancy loss. aCL and anti-β2-GP1 antibodies. Lupus anticoagulant.
Q13

c-ANCA positivity indicates, antibody formed against: Proteinase 3 Myeloperoxidase Cytoplasmic antinuclear antibody Anti centromere antibody

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

APS: prolonged aPTT (lupus anticoagulant) but clinically THROMBOTIC.
Q14

p-ANCA is characteristic for: Henoch-Schonlein purpura

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Sjögren's: dry eyes + dry mouth. Anti-Ro (SS-A) and anti-La (SS-B). Risk of MALT lymphoma.
Q15

The presence of anti-saccharomyces cerevisae antibody is a surrogate marker of one of the following: * Coeliac disease Crohn's disease Ulcerative colitis Tropical sprue

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Schirmer's test: <5 mm in 5 min = dry eyes (Sjögren's).
Q16

Antiendomysial Antibody is typically seen in: Collagenous Colitis

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Systemic sclerosis: diffuse (anti-Scl-70) vs limited (anti-centromere, CREST). Raynaud's in almost all.
Q17

All of the following condition are associated with granulomatous pathology, except: Wegner's Granulomatosis (WG) Takayasu Arteritis (TA) Polyarteritis Nodosa (Classic PAN) Giant Cell Arteritis (GCA)

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

CREST: Calcinosis + Raynaud's + oEsophageal dysmotility + Sclerodactyly + Telangiectasia. Anti-centromere Ab.
Q18

A person with involvement of upper respiratory tract, lungs and kidney shows evidence of granulomas on histo pathology. The most likely diaglosis is: * Goodpasture Syndrome

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Diffuse systemic sclerosis: anti-Scl-70. ILD and PAH are leading causes of death.
Q19

A 20 year old woman presents with bilateral conductive deafness, palpable purpura on the legs and hemoptysis. Radiograph of the chest shows a thin-walled cavity in left lower zone. Investigations reveal total leukocyte count 12000/mm³, red cell casts in the urine and serum creatinine 3mg/dL. What is the most probable diagnosis?

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Raynaud's: white → blue → red (vasospasm → cyanosis → reperfusion).
Q20

A 25 years old female develops serous otitis media of left ear with cough and occasional hemoptysis and hematuria and epistaxis for one and half months her. Hemoglobin is 7 gm. B.P. > 170/100, ptoreinuria +++, RA positive (+ve) and ANCA positive (+ve), the likely cause is- * Wegener's granulo matosis Rheumiatoid arthritis Rapidly proliferative glomerulonephritis Good pasteur's syndrome

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Dermatomyositis: heliotrope rash + Gottron's papules + proximal muscle weakness. CK elevated.
Q22

Wegener's granulomatosis does not affect: Kidney Lungs Eye Liver

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Polymyositis: proximal muscle weakness, elevated CK. No skin rash. CD8+ T cells in endomysium.
Q23

Cavitating lesion in lung is seen in: Sjogern's syndrome

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Jo-1 antibody: PM/DM with interstitial lung disease (antisynthetase syndrome).
Q24

Drug of choice to induce remission in Severe Wegner's Granulomatosis is Azathioprine Etanercept

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

MCTD: overlap SLE + PM + systemic sclerosis. Anti-U1-RNP antibody.
Q25

In polyarteritis nodosa, aneurysms are seen in all, except: Lung Pancreas

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Gout: monosodium urate crystals (needle-shaped, negatively birefringent). 1st MTP joint most common.
Q26

A 30 years old male patient presents with complaints of weakness in right upper and both lower limbs of last 4 months. He developed digital infarcts involving 2nd and 3rd fingers on right side and 5th finger on left side. On examination, BP was 160/140 mm Hg, all peripheral pulses were palpable and there was asymmetrical neuropathy. Investigations showed a Hb 12 gm, TLC-12000 Cu mm, Platelets 4,30,000, ESR-49 mm. Urine examination showed proteinuria and RBC – 10-15/hpf with no casts. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Gout treatment: acute = indomethacin/colchicine/steroids. Chronic prophylaxis = allopurinol (xanthine oxidase inhibitor).
Q27

An 18 year old boy presents with digital gangrene in 3rd & 4th fingers for last 2 weeks. On examination the BP is 170/110 mm of Hg and all peripheral pulses were palpable. Blood & Urine examination were unremarkable. Antinuclear antibody, Antibody to ds DNA & DNA & ANCA were negative. Most likely diagnosis is:

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Pseudogout (CPPD): calcium pyrophosphate crystals (rhomboid, weakly positively birefringent). Knees most common.
Q28

A 30 year old male presents with numbness of both lower limbs and right upper limb. Examination reveals pulse 88/minutes and BPI 160/110 mm of Hg. He also has digital gangrene involving right 2nd and 3rd finger, urine routine examination is unremarkable. Microscopic examination shows RBC's. Hemogram and serum biochemistry is within normal limits. What is the most probable diagnosis? * Chugh-Strauss syndrome

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Reactive arthritis (Reiter's syndrome): urethritis + conjunctivitis + arthritis. Post-chlamydial (STI) or post-enteric (Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter).
Q29

A patient presents with melaena normal renal function, hypertension and mononeuritis multiplex. The most probable diagnosis is: * Classical polyarteritis nodosa Buerger's disease

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Ankylosing spondylitis: HLA-B27, sacroiliitis, bamboo spine on X-ray, 'question mark' posture. Treated: NSAIDs, anti-TNF.
Q30

Biopsy in PAN shows: Necrotizing arteritis

Psoriatic arthritis: asymmetric, DIP joint involvement, 'sausage digits' (dactylitis), nail pitting.
Q31

Which of the following is not true about Churg Strauss Syndrome Asthma Peripheral eosinophilia Vasculitis of multiple organ systems Intravascular Granulomas

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Behcet's disease: recurrent oral ulcers + genital ulcers + uveitis + skin lesions. HLA-B51. Pathergy test positive.
Q32

All of the following statements about Churg Strauss Syndrome are true, except: Marked Eosinophilia Mononeuritis multiplex is common Renal failure is common Often associated with Allergic Rhinitis

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Giant cell arteritis (temporal arteritis): elderly, headache, jaw claudication, vision loss (anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy), elevated ESR/CRP. Treat: high-dose prednisolone immediately.
Q33

Triad of tissue eosinophilia, granulomatous inflammation, and vasculitis is typical of Alport's syndrome Churg-Strauss syndrome Cyoglobulinemia

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR): elderly, proximal muscle pain + stiffness, elevated ESR. Associated with GCA. Responds dramatically to low-dose prednisolone.
Q34

Clinical triad of Asthma, Peripheral Eosinophilia and Mononeuritis multiplex suggests a diagnosis of

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Wegener's granulomatosis (GPA): c-ANCA/PR3. Saddle-nose deformity, pulmonary nodules/cavities, crescentic GN. Treat: cyclophosphamide + prednisolone.
Q35

Which of the following is more frequently seen in Churg Strauss Syndrome in comparison to Wegener's Granulomatosis * Renal involvement Lower Respiratory Tract involvement Eye involvement Upper Respiratory Tract involvement

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Polyarteritis nodosa (PAN): medium vessel vasculitis, spares lungs, mononeuritis multiplex, renal involvement. p-ANCA negative. Associated with HBV.
Q36

Feature of microscopic polyangiitis is: IgG deposits in kidney Bronchospasm Renal involvement in 80% of cases

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Churg-Strauss (EGPA): asthma + eosinophilia + p-ANCA/MPO. Granulomatous inflammation of small-medium vessels.
Q37

All of the following features may be used to distinguish PAN from microscopic Polyangitis, Except: * ANCA positivity RBC cast in urine Necritizing vasculitis HBV infection

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Kawasaki disease: fever >5 days + 4 of 5: bilateral conjunctival injection, lip/oral changes, cervical lymphadenopathy, polymorphous rash, changes in extremities. Risk of coronary aneurysm. Treat: IV immunoglobulin + aspirin.
Q38

All of the following are true about Temporal Arteritis except : Polymyalgia Rheumatica Low ESR Sudden blindness

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Henoch-Schönlein purpura (IgA vasculitis): palpable purpura (buttocks/legs) + arthritis + abdominal pain + renal involvement (haematuria). Most common vasculitis in children.
Q39

An elderly female presents to the emergency department with history of fever, headache and double vision. Biopsy of temporal artery revealed panarteritis. The most likely diagnosis is * Nonspecific Arteritis Temporal Arteritis

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Takayasu's arteritis: granulomatous aortoarteritis in young Asian women. Pulseless disease. Elevated ESR, ANA may be positive.
Q40

Which of the following is the most frequent presenting symptoms in patients with giant cell arteritis * Jaw claudication Polymyalgin Rheumatica Blindness

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Antiphospholipid syndrome: triple positivity (lupus anticoagulant + aCL + anti-β2-GP1) = highest thrombotic risk.
Q41

A 52 year old lady presents with throbbing continuous headache on both sides of the forehead that gets worse while chewing. The likely diagnosis is: * Migraine Cluster headache Takayasu Arteritis Giant Cell Arteritis

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Sjogren's secondary: associated with RA, SLE, systemic sclerosis. Primary Sjogren's is isolated.
Q42

Jaw tightness is typically seen in: Coarctation of aorta Giant cell arteritis Wegner's Granulomatosis

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Neonatal lupus: from maternal anti-Ro/SS-A → transient rash + cytopenia + congenital complete heart block (permanent).
Q43

All of the following statements about temporal arteritis are true, Except: More common in females Worsens on exposure to heat Seen in elderly women Can lead to sudden bilateral blindness

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Systemic sclerosis renal crisis: sudden malignant HTN + AKI. Treat: ACE inhibitors (DO NOT withhold even if ACE-I 'contraindicated' in other contexts of AKI here).
Q44

The investigation of choice for diagnosis of Giant cell Arteritis is Temporal Artery biopsy Colour Doppler of Temporal Artery CT Angiography MRI

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Marfan's syndrome: FBN1 gene (fibrillin-1). Tall, arachnodactyly, lens dislocation (upward), aortic root dilation/AR, MVP.
Q45

All of the following statements about Polymyalgia Rheumatica are true, except Common in young females Bilateral Shoulder Pain Elevated ESR Morning stiffness

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Ehlers-Danlos syndrome: collagen defect. Hyperextensible skin, hypermobile joints, fragile blood vessels.
Q46

All of the following are true about Polymyalgia Rheumatica Except: Positive RA Factor Morning Stiffness Rapid Response to Low Dose Corticosteroids

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Osteogenesis imperfecta: type I collagen defect. Blue sclerae, brittle bones, hearing loss, dentinogenesis imperfecta.
Q47

All of the following are true about Polymyalgia Rheumatica (PMR), except: Inflammatory Rheumatic Disease May be seen in 40–50% of patients with giant cell arteritis. 10 to 20 % of patients with isolated PMR may progress to Giant Cell Arteritis Poor Prognosis

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Homocystinuria: CBS (cystathionine beta synthase) deficiency. Lens dislocation (downward, vs Marfan's upward), marfanoid habitus, thromboembolism, intellectual disability.
Q48

A young female presents with diminished pulses in the upper limb and hypertension. The most likely Coarctation of Aorta

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Alkaptonuria: HGO (homogentisate oxidase) deficiency. Black pigmented urine on standing, ochronosis (blue-black pigment in connective tissue), arthropathy.
Q49

Bilateral upper limb pulse less disease is? Giant cell Arteritis Aortoarteritis

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Achondroplasia: FGFR3 gain-of-function mutation. AD. Short stature, rhizomelic dwarfism, large head, normal intelligence.
Q50

Reversed Coarctation is seen in: Kawasaki Disease

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Thanatophoric dysplasia: FGFR3 mutation. Lethal skeletal dysplasia.
Q51

Takayasu arteritis mainly affects? Pulmonary artery Celiac artery Subclavian artery SMA

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Fibrodysplasia ossificans progressiva (FOP): ectopic ossification. ACVR1 (ALK2) mutation. Starts in neck/shoulder then progresses. 'Second skeleton'.
Q52

Most common variant of Takayasu Disease in India is: Type-1 Type-2 Type-3 Type-4

Options not available in source. Refer to textbook.

Mixed connective tissue disease (MCTD): anti-U1-snRNP antibodies. Overlapping features of SLE, PM, and systemic sclerosis.
